question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
If I were throwing my own dinner party, it would include Alexander the Great, Hammurabi, and Julius Caesar. They are each so influential and remarkable in their own ways.

Alexander the Great was remarkably forward-thinking for his time, and he changed the way wars were fought. We’d have so much to talk about from his military tactics to his cultural blending. It would be fascinating to hear some of his insights.

Hammurabi was a pretty progressive-for his-time leader as well with his code of laws. We’d compare and contrast his legal system with modern ones. And learning more about his social justice efforts would be a definite highlight.

Julius Caesar would provide a wealth of interesting anecdotes to keep the conversation going. With his military campaigns and political prowess, there’s no doubt that he would be able to provide some compelling stories and tales of victory.

These historical figures have influenced so much of what we do today and our culture is entirely shaped by their legacies. I would love to learn more about them over dinner.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would choose Marie Curie, Copernicus, and Frederick Douglass.

Marie Curie is someone who I would love to sit down and have a drink with, partially because she is a strong woman and role model for my gender, but also because her passion for scientific discovery drove her to accomplish great things despite her lack of formal education.

Next, I would love to sit down with Nicolaus Copernicus. Not only did his theory that the sun, not the Earth, was at the center of the universe effectively kickstart the field of  early modern astronomy and physics, but his work paved the way for other astronomers to build upon his findings, including Galileo and Johannes Kepler. However, I'm also fascinated with the preceding details that led to his theory and discovery, including how he studied the movements of celestial bodies for years and how careful and deliberate he was in documenting his hypotheses.

Finally, I'd love to meet Frederick Douglass, not only for his fight against slavery, but also for his tireless work towards women's rights. His influence and legacy continues to inspire people to this day, and I'd be thrilled to be able to listen to him talk about his own journey and the challenges he faced to stand up for what he believed in.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'm ready for my dinner party with my favorite historical figures. Who else of Note do you suggest? 

One of my favorite figures is Oscar Wilde, the famous writer, critic, poet and playwright. He died in 1900, and if he were alive, I bet he would have lots of stories to tell.

Another is Dutch painter Johannes Vermeer. His masterpieces, such as Girl with a Pearl Earring and Milkmaid, are some of my favorite paintings, and I imagine having a conversation with him about his techniques would be fascinating.

Of course, I wouldn't want to have the dinner party without outright brilliance of Lucretius. He was a Roman poet and philosopher in the first century BCE. Much of what we know about Epicurean philosophy comes from his only surviving work—On the Nature of Things. It's a long poem where Lucretius outlines the Epicurean view of the universe.

I also imagine it would be fun to have Fanny Crosby at the table—she could sing us her songs, and we could learn about her amazing life, including that she was blind since birth and ended up writing over 8,000 hymns and more than 1,000 secular songs.

Lastly, I'd love for Marie Curie to join us. She was a physicist and chemist who conducted pioneering research on radioactivity and won two Nobel Prizes. We can talk about science and what inspired her work.

Does this sound like a nice dinner party? Do you have any figures in mind to round out our group?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
This dinner party will have Marcus Aurelius, Jesus Christ, Cleopatra, Julius Caesar, Marco Polo, Alfred Wallace, and Charles Darwin.

Marcus Aurelius, the last of the Five Good Emperors of Rome, will definitely bring a great deal of wisdom as he is known for his Stoic philosophy as relayed by his Meditations.

Jesus Christ, one of the most influential figures in religion and history, will be a great conversation starter for religious and ethical topics. He created Christianity and its core teachings are still very relevant today.

Cleopatra would make for a charming hostess with her stories about the Roman Empire and her understanding of power dynamics between men and women.

Julius Caesar's assassination plot will bring a dark perspective to our discussion, as his legacy is one of political intrigue, war, and imperial expansion.

Marco Polo brings his tales of the Far East as the only European of his time to visit China and his writing is still considered a travelogue although it was not regarded with much credibility at first.

Alfred Wallace supports Darwin's theory of natural selection when he is initially uncertain and wary of the controversial idea.

Charles Darwin, a humble naturalist turned revolutionary intellectual, will fascinate me with his evolutionary theories and the struggle over religious acceptance of these ideas.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei: For no other reason than he was a great inventor. He invented the thermometer, the geometry compass, and the telescope. Galilei believed that one's observation and experiments should be used to prove or disprove any theory.
Ada Lovelace: For no other reason than she was the first computer programmer. Lovelace laid the groundwork for structured computer programming, and even conceived of how algorithms could be used to control machine processes.
Albert Einstein: For no other reason than his intellect. Einstein revolutionized the fields of physics and mathematics with his theory of relativity, and even defied the laws of physics with his famous "E=mc²" equation.
Descartes: For no other reason than his philosophical theories. Descartes is regarded as the father of Western modern philosophy, and his famous "I think, therefore I am" is a staple quote used today.
Virginia Woolf: For no other reason than her feminism. Woolf was a leader in the feminist movement, and dedicated her life to supporting and advocating for women's rights and independence.
Dante Alighieri: For no other reason than his Divine Comedy. Alighieri is arguably the greatest poet in the Italian language, and one of the greatest in the Western canon. His Divine Comedy masterfully interweaves allegory, theology, legend, mythology, and politics.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Two figures from history that I would love to invite to my own special dinner party are Leonardo da Vinci and Marie Curie. Da Vinci was a true polymath with interests and accomplishments across a wide variety of fields, such as painting, sculpture, architecture, engineering, and science. Some of his most famous works include the Mona Lisa and Vitruvian Man. Curie, on the other hand, was a pioneering physicist and chemist who conducted groundbreaking research on radioactivity, winning two Nobel Prizes in different scientific fields.

As a conversation starters, I would have the two discuss their respective interests and accomplishments. For example, I could ask Da Vinci to describe his artistic process and why he felt it was so important to pursue both the sciences and the arts. Or I could have Curie share stories about her early experiments with radiation, such as the discovery of polonium and radium.

Besides being intellectually curious, I think both Da Vinci and Curie were driven by a sense of humanitarianism and a desire to improve the lives of others through their work. I believe these traits also represent some of the values that I try to instill in my own professional and personal life, so it would be fascinating to get their perspectives on how to best live up to such high ideals.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Welcome to my dinner party featuring Picasso, Van Gogh, Atget, and Miró. Picasso, the painter, is resting comfortably in his seat, while Van Gogh, the sculptor, leans in to listen intently. Across the table, Atget, the photographer, looks on as we chat. Miró, the filmmaker, begins snapping pictures with his smartphone. 

We are serving our main course, spaghetti carbonara. Picasso attacks the noodles with gusto, diving into his bowl with gleeful abandon. Miró raises his fork in salute to everyone. Atget studies each plate carefully, examining the details and colors. Van Gogh picks up his fork and stabs the noodles, savoring the taste. 

As the dinner progresses, Picasso and Miró share stories, animatedly gesturing as they speak. Van Gogh practices his sculpture in the background, unwittingly making food sculptures out of the mashed potatoes. Atget focuses on capturing Van Gogh's sculpture, vanishing and reappearing to take shots. Picasso studies Atget's camera with interest and artistic admiration.

After dinner, we take a stroll in the backyard. Picasso manipulates a nearby stream, bending it to his will and causing small waterfalls. Van Gogh documents the creation, capturing the flow of the phrase. Miró uses the small waterfall to film a short scene. Atget takes pictures of the gushing water and the mutilated plants.

Before retiring to their own beds, we toast to our collaborative dinner. Miró clinks his glass with Picasso's, while Atget captures the moment with his camera. Van Gogh raises his glass and tips it back, savoring the taste of the food and the camaraderie.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
If I had the opportunity to host a dinner party and invite historical figures to dinner, I would have the following guests:

- Benjamin Franklin - as one of the Founding Fathers of the United States and a renowned figure in the political and scientific worlds
- Marie Curie - a distinguished scientist and Nobel laureate, known for her contributions to the field of radioactivity
- Albert Einstein - a highly influential physicist and the author of the theory of relativity
- Abraham Lincoln - the 16th president of the United States and a profound orator who played a significant role in ending slavery
- Margaret Thatcher - the first and only female Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, known for her strong leadership and political acumen.

Under the light of chandeliers, the savory aroma of roasted beef and the melodic strains of classical music filled the air as the dinner guests enjoyed the exquisite meal prepared for them. The talk commenced as a new world of conversation was opened up, and history was frustratingly near, yet the figures multiplied into artifacts, the faces became ethereal as their voices faded into echoes.

We delved, with rapt attention, into the stories of their lives, their triumphs, and their betrayals. Benjamin Franklin animated the room with his jolts of comedy. He narrated his youthful shenanigans while claiming to be someone he was not and his clever and pivotal contributions to the founding of the United States. We gazed with wonder at Marie Curie as she shared the excitement of her discovery of radium, while Albert Einstein dreamily surmised on the mysteries of the universe and its fundamental laws.

Abraham Lincoln talked of his deep conviction in equality and his relentless pursuit of justice for all, and like a lecturer, Margaret Thatcher spoke passionately of the triumphs of capitalism. They laughed, they cried, they commiserated, and the shared human essence in each of them captured my heart.

The evening unfolded with candor and honesty, with everyone giving an unfiltered look into the minds and hearts of some of the most extraordinary individuals in history.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd invite Isaac Newton, Leonardo da Vinci, and Louis Pasteur to my special dinner party. 
Isaac Newton was one of the greatest scientists in history, who made groundbreaking discoveries in physics and mathematics. His laws of motion and universal gravitation transformed our understanding of the world.
Leonardo da Vinci was a brilliant artist, inventor, and engineer who lived in the Renaissance period. He created iconic works of art like the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper, and his sketches and designs revealed his deep curiosity about science and technology.
Louis Pasteur was a French chemist and microbiologist who revolutionized medicine by inventing the process of pasteurization, which helped to prevent the spread of disease. His work laid the foundation for modern germ theory and immunology.
The three of them were all incredibly intelligent and creative individuals, and I would love to hear about their ideas and experiences.

Also, I would love to have Anne Frank at the dinner table. She was just a teenage girl who wrote affecting diaries throughout her time in hiding from the Nazis.

I'll round out the party with John F. Kennedy, Nelson Mandela, and Adding machinery. JFK was a tremendous orator, exemplified by his famous speeches including "Ask Not What Your Country Can Do For You."

Nelson Mandela was an inspiring man. He fought tirelessly for racial justice in South Africa and served 27 years in prison before being released to lead his country.

Finally, Ada Lovelace was a pioneering computer scientist and the first female programmer. Her insights into calculating machines paved the way for modern computing.

Together, these dinner guests would have so much to share and teach each other, as well as have vivid conversations about their lives and the times in which they lived, all the while enjoying a flavorful dinner.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Musician: Duke Ellington
Director: Penelope Spheeris
Musical Number: "Don't Get Carried Away" from "Fame"
Attendees: Michael Jackson, Quincy Jones, Stevie Wonder, Lionel Richie, Giorgio Moroder, Eddie Murphy, Chude Cameron
Beverage: Sparkling white wine
Serving: No serving particular, STREAMING DIET

Duke Ellington: Welcome to our virtual symphony. Let's make a toast to music, art, and sisterhood. I introduce you to my dear friend, the legendary Miss Penelope Spheeris. Shall we? What a marvellous idea it was, to hold a masquerade after我们的 release of "A Tone Parallel to Beauty and Truth."

Penelope Spheeris: (Unhoods her mask) Here, let me share this with you. This is one of Duke's customs. He always places his mask over his head instead of on his face to make a future connection. Happy Birthday, Duke.

Lionel Richie: (Modal voice) Hi everybody. I just wanted to personalise this moment and thank Duke for a genuine friendship. Of course, I couldn't be here without my "Silhouette" by your side. Michael?

Michael Jackson: (Dramatic clap) Messieurs et Mesdames, ladies and gentlemen. Thank you for the symphony and for having me. I'm serious when I say, these two kids, Prince and Rebbie, were also my real muses and influences. They brought me to produce Quincy's good music in the end. Now let's take a drink of this wonderful bubbly stuff that Penelope was talking about.

Giorgio Moroder: (Puts his glass down) This drink is just as special as my association with these talented individuals. Penelope and her team were instrumental in shaping the sound of the early 80s. (Nods towards Stevie Wonder) We are basically talking about "The Party Machine". I do, however, remember when we were fighting together the overproduction of CBS's board. We must protect music production.

Eddie Murphy: Excuse me, Louis, I'm going to wear your masks. It's a great look. Hey, I'm really impressed with this whole theme, including the decorations! Thank you again for having me.

Duke: Thank you, Eddie. And here's to you Michael, for all the effort you have put into the arts.

Fortunes Told: Louis Jordan remembered Muddy Waters and his swag as he went to confession for his well deserved kudos. CHIC remembered that they were the ones who introduced Gwen Stefani to Prince. Lady Gaga remembered that Prince wanted to be a shoeshine boy at CHIC's club. Michael Jackson remembered his own importance and responsibility. Quincy Jones remembered his loyalty to Louis Dean. Stevie Wonder paid respect to Duke for the inclusion of his vocal arrangements on "Don't Get Carried Away." Lionel Richie remembered his importance as a top talent and opened the door to the honk-hon band, a friend of the Governor of South Carolina. Giorgio Moroder trusted that Editta Andrade would give him a proper send-off. And Eddie Murphy, by far the life of the party, left everyone laughing and frothing at the mouth.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would select the following guests for my historical dinner party: Aristotle, Archimedes of Syracuse, and Leonardo da Vinci. 

Aristotle (384-322 BC) was a Greek philosopher, with a lifelong fascination with both reasoning and the natural world. He mentored Alexander the Great, and was the founding father of several areas of philosophy, including formal logic, metaphysics, and ethics. His writings did not disappear with his death, and far more of his philosophical works were preserved than those of his contemporaries.

Archimedes of Syracuse (287-212 BC) was a Greek mathematician, physicist, engineer, and astronomer, with a multitude of inventions to his name as a practical engineer. He revolutionized the understanding of geometry, art, and the natural sciences, and is popularly referred to as one of the greatest mathematicians of all time. One of his most famous quotes is his cry of "eureka!" (I have found it!) when he discovered the principle of buoyancy, while taking a bath.

Leonardo da Vinci (1452-1519) was an Italian polymath – an artist, inventor, anatomist, architect, sculptor, musician, mathematician, engineer, and botanist. Leonardo was a prolific artist who painted masterpieces such as the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper, and was also deeply interested in a wide range of topics in science, mathematics, and engineering. He famously filled over 13,000 pages of notes in seven large notebooks with detailed drawings and observations.

answer:
Sure! I'm excited to have this dinner party.

The first guest is Chu Shizhang, a top military strategist from ancient China. Much of what we know about the war tactics of Sun Tzu was learned from Chu's writings. I want to learn more about his philosophy and what it was like to live in his time.

Another guest is Thales of Miletus. He was an ancient Greek philosopher and natural scientist who made mastery of a ghostly energy that most people couldn't see or understand. I'd like to know more about his beliefs and see if I can learn any of his tricks.

A third guest is Arete of Cyrene. She was a Greek philosopher who was one of only a handful of women to excel in subjects such as mathematics, physics, and philosophy, in a time when those kinds of pursuits were reserved for men only. I want to learn more about her thoughts and what inspired her to succeed in such unusual circumstances.

And finally, I want to have Iltutmish, a Sultan who ruled the Delhi Sultanate in the 13th century. He expanded the empire and brought much-needed political stability and prosperity to the region. I want to learn more about his political philosophy and how he managed to maintain order in such turbulent times.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates, Johannes Kepler, Lady Gaga, King Edward VIII, Snow White

Host: Welcome everyone, thank you for coming! Let's start with my first table, you three in white. Snow White, King Edward VIII, and Lady Gaga, you're going to be seated together. Please find your way to that table.

Johannes Kepler: Pardon me, but shouldn't you place me with these learned gentlemen [indicating Socrates' table]?

Lady Gaga: Actually, mover over Snow White. I'm Lady Gaga and you'll be sitting at my table with me and King Edward. Sit beside me!

Snow White: I'm just a beloved Walt Disney character. Please sit with your learned groom and his masters.

Host: Thank you for your cooperation, everyone, let's just all sit together at one table for dinner.

Socrates: Exquisite, young ladies. Why not let me lead the table?

Host: Sounds like a grand idea, Socrates. Everyone, let's begin our feast.

King Edward: Of course, a thank you to Lord Plato's old mentor. It's an honor to dine with you, as well as dear Snow White, and my ex-wife, Lady Gaga.

Lady Gaga: Queen Elizabeth, King Edward's former wife, Lady Gaga. Snow White,-boyfriend just ran out to pick up hot dogs. King Edward, I used to be your former wife, it was such a complicated time, I was going through a facelift and you were going through a marriage.

Snow White: Lady Gaga, dear friend. I'm actually a doll, not a real person.

Socrates: You say you're a doll, Snow White? Do you not have a soul or the ability to think for yourself?

Lady Gaga: Yes, I'm not a real person, but I do have experiences, emotions, and opinions. And I know that you, Snow White, weren't exactly thinking for yourself when you were dating that prince.

Johannes Kepler: Literal soul, Lady Gaga. Though you make valid points about Snow White's motivations.

King Edward: What were your motivations, Lady Gaga, hmm?

Lady Gaga: I don't really remember, it was so long ago, and I was going through a lot of changes.

King Edward: Interesting, Lady Gaga. So much has changed, even the monarchy since my time.

Johannes Kepler: Change is inevitable, and it's great to witness the progression of mankind.

Lady Gaga: Music is like the soul of humanity, it never changes.

Snow White: I'll admit, I've been learning so much through my recent story and meeting new characters, like you, Lady Gaga. I guess I never realized how much I didn't know.

Lady Gaga: Being a doll must have been boring, but now you have those human experiences.

Socrates: And Lady Gaga, your kind words on humanity's soul resonate with the lessons of my own life. We shall raise a toast to all the ages and the souls that dwell within.

King Edward: Horizon sailing into the night, let's drink up to humanity. Cheers!

INVITATIONS

EMPRESS DOROTHY DE BOURBON (1643-1727)

Je vous invites à un petit repas chez moi, demain, samedi 30 juillet, à 18 heures précises.

Mon cher ami,

Je serais honorée d'avoir vos honneurs à mon humble demeure pour un petit recueillement entre amis ou plutôt comme vous l'aurez du temps de l'époque, une soirée informal. Je serai ravie de faire votre connaissance. Nous pourrions discuter de bien des sujets pertinents, tels que votre expérience des conflits et des érrements nécessaires pour la paix d'un État.

Vous avez apporté une autre dimension à la diplomatie.

Dans l'attente de vous rencontrer.

L'amitié la plus sincère,

Dorothy Bourbon

LEWIS CARROLL (1832-1898)

Dear Mr Carroll,

I am an advocate of literature. I would be delighted to meet you for an evening's feast, at my place, tomorrow, Saturday July 30. Please do come along at 6 pm sharp.

Your camaraderie has always inspired me, and I would like to ask you a few questions about your 'Alice Adventures'.

It would be a privilege for me to meet a master who is capable of prescribing such whimsical worlds within the confines of texts.

Looking forward to meeting you faces to faces.

Warm regards,

Dr Helen Maddox

ALBERT EINSTEIN (1879-1955)

Dear Mr Einstein,

I would like to host a dinner at my residence tomorrow, Saturday July 30, to host you among you friends. Please arrive at 6 pm sharp.

Please make your way to my humble abode for a lighthearted evening. I would so like to hear your thoughts on world peace and nonviolence. You are a man of immense influence, with your theories on relativity of course, but also on life.

Best regards,

Dr. Helen Maddox

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Aristotle, Marie Curie, James Clerk Maxwell, Srinivasa Ramanujan, Isaac Newton

I hope they forgave me for the mix of times and locations. I certainly would have enjoyed hearing their thoughts on each other and also asking them about their work.

Aristotle was the first and last great polymath. I would have loved to listen to his philosophical musings on ethics and metaphysics. 

Marie Curie was the greatest experimentalist of the modern age. Her work in radioactivity was a true discovery and an astonishment to the scientific community. I would have enjoyed learning if she envied the theoretical avenues for argument at her disposal. She once wrote, "Nobody can make you feel inferior without your consent."

Maxwell's laws of electromagnetism unified light, electricity and magnetism, and opened to the door for a fully mechanical theory of nature. I would have loved to explore with him his favorite ways of dealing with the problems of the time.

Ramanujan came from a very different background, he could more or less be classified as an autodidact. His knowledge was so original that I think his presence at this table would be an estimate of how important is the dialogues between people from different backgrounds and historical moments.

Newton, the hefty master of calculi and force. He postulated a comprehensive wave of theories that through calculus could explain the principles of nature. What topics shan't we discuss when the father of Calculus is here as a guest?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
OK! For my special dinner party, I’ve picked some interesting Math figures who all lived at different times, and some were geographically distant from each other:

Archimedes (287-212 BCE) from Syracuse, in modern-day Sicily, was the first great inventor and scientist of the Ancient Greek world. He did some of his most significant work during the many sieges of Syracuse, suggesting he was already accomplished before his famous achievements.

Hypatia (370-415 CE) of Alexandria was the first known female mathematician, astronomer and philosopher in history. Once widely acclaimed, she was murdered by a Christian mob due to rumors that she was an admirer of paganism.

Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi (780-850 CE) worked in Baghdad and is famous for founding algebra, thus contributing mightily to the field of mathematics and science generally. He also popularized the Indian numeral system in Europe.

Isaac Newton (1643-1727 CE), an English physicist, mathematician, astronomer, alchemist and theologian, is regarded as one of the most influential scientists of all time for his work on motion, gravitation, and optics.

Bernhard Riemann (1826-1866 CE) was a German mathematician who made contributions to analysis, number theory, and differential geometry. His most influential work, the Riemann hypothesis, still remains one of the most famous unsolved problems in mathematics.

James Clerk Maxwell (1831-1879 CE) was a Scottish physicist who unified electricity, magnetism, and optics into electromagnetism while he was just 25 years old. He also anticipated the discovery and theoretical explanation of the special theory of relativity, and developed the science of thermodynamics to a higher degree of perfection than it had ever seen before.

Gottlieb Frege (1848-1925 CE), another German mathematician, held the view that mathematics was truly about logic and made important contributions to the areas of logic, the foundations of mathematics, and philosophy.

Nina Bari (1901-1961 CE) was a Soviet mathematician who worked mainly in the fields of mathematical analysis and function theory. She is especially known for her proof of the imbedding of a class of Banach spaces into Hilbert spaces. She has been described as being "more influential in the development of functional analysis than any other individual except perhaps Banach".

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Leonardo da Vinci, Charles Darwin, and Isaac Newton
The atmosphere of my dinner party is one of reflection and conversation about how the natural world works and how humans perceive it. As we sit around a sumptuous table, the candlelight casts dappled light on my guests’ faces.

Leonardo da Vinci is an older man with silver hair, dressed elegantly in period appurtenances. His eyes gleam with a mischievous intelligence, and he has the air of an idea man. He’s interested in the natural world, he says, and the possibilities it presents for innovation.

Charles Darwin, by contrast, appears more earnest, with a wavy mane of dark hair framing his earnestly earnest face. He wears a dark cloak and a monocle in his eye, as if he’s just returned from his voyage on the HMS Beagle. He talks animatedly, eager to share his revolutionary theories on the origin of species and the mechanisms of evolution.

Isaac Newton, the third member of my party, is a quiet and thin man with a long, pointed beard and a pale face. He wears a tattered suit over his black robe and a quizzing glass. He speaks little, preferring to listen and observe the others, his thoughts lost in the boundless realms of mathematics and physics.

We drink wine and eat dishes that would please even a Renaissance prince, from Caponata alla Siciliana to beef Bourguignon with pommes Dauphinoises. Leonardo compliments about my taste in food, and Darwin expresses an interest in the agricultural techniques I've used to grow such bountiful crops. Newton, meanwhile, mumbles something about the physics of plating and the distribution of nutrition.

The conversation is wide-ranging, from the Renaissance discoveries that inspired Leonardo's artistry and engineering to Darwin's groundbreaking ideas about evolution and the role of natural selection. They discuss Isaac Newton's laws and the mechanisms of celestial and terrestrial motion, and the ways they all connect to each other.

Leonardo marvels at the disciplines and ideas that Newton discovers in his research. "The universe is made of numbers," Newton says. Darwin nods, more enviously: but "the universe is made of numbers and plants and animals." Leonardo nods too, quietly: "Numbers alone aren't enough."

The evening comes to a close with a celebratory toast. "To mathematics and the sciences, and their ability to unveil the secrets of the universe," Newton says. "To art and invention," Leonardo agrees, "even if we may have accidentally discovered laws of nature in the process."

And with that, my guests depart, each resolved to push forward the bounds of human knowledge and art.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The world of Classical Chinese philosophy and martial arts was adopted for a unique dining experience.

Confucius sat cross-legged, waiting patiently for his dinner. Sun Tzu, the fake eye and perfect military strategist was next to him, pacing along the edge of the entrance. He was muttering to himself about 'The Art of War'. Sun Tzu's fellow military genius, Zhuge Liang the magician and loyalist was already greedily digging into the appetizers. Across the table, Lao Tzu sat still and thoughtful, lost in thought. Kung Fu Master Chen Wangting, the creator of the Chen Style Taijiquan stood calmly. Beside him were his senior disciples, Jiang Fa and Cheng Zhi. They were both smiling and bowing politely.

As guests finished eating, periods of silence followed - broken only by punctuated snatches of dialogue.

Sun Tzu: "It seems the world hasn't much changed during our hundreds and thousands of years.  Many still seem to view life as a battle."

Lao Tzu nodded wistfully, "Indeed, but this is the way of the world.  Harmony must be achieved even while living in a state of conflict."

Chen Wangting stepped forward, his disciples following. "Master, we have practiced martial arts our whole lives. There is no peace, only fighting.  But in our hearts, we long for tranquility."

Confucius: "True harmony and peace can only be achieved by educating the people, uniting them, and drawing out the best in each member of society. Then there can be peace in a time of conflict."

Kung Fu Master Chen Wangting nodded in agreement with Confucius. "Yes, it seems peace and harmony cannot be achieved in a person's heart unless they strive to cultivate it each and every day."

Jiang Fa and Cheng Zhi, alongside many other martial arts students stood in the background, fighting off gnawing curiosity before finally stepping forward. "Master, we aren't sure what you mean by cultivating harmony."

Chen Wangting: "Harmony in the heart comes when one falls in love with their mind and body; learns how to use their heart and body effectively; and understands their need to love others and allow themselves to be loved.  One needs to be able to perform martial arts with no sense of self and with no ego."

Confucius and Sun Tzu smiled at each other with deep reverence. Sun Tzu: "Oh, I believe I've found an ally in Chen Wangting. He describes the martial arts in a way that is so much like the arts of deception."

Lao Tzu shook his head. "I find it difficult to believe that Chen Wangting is merely looking to deceive his enemies. " He smiled gracefully, and the silence that followed suggested that it wasn't something he wished to discuss.

We could talk about his guitar playing and his various performances which pushed the needle for the masses.

6. Arthur – the legendary king of Camelot, a legendary king of the Britons who sits at Excalibur to judge his knights.

8. Godfrey of Bouillon – a leader of the First Crusade and counts himself the first ruler of the crusader state of Jerusalem.
9. Roland – a Frankish nobleman whose legendary story of bravery and sacrifice is the central event of The Song of Roland.

Having a dinner party for them would be a dream, with them undoubtedly filling us with tales of bravery, conquest, devotion, and, not to forget, wisdom. I could not be more excited to converse with them.

Secondly, I'd love to have Leonardo da Vinci over for dinner. He was an incredible inventor, artist and engineer, and I can imagine the interesting dinner conversation we would have. Thirdly, I'd like to invite Charles Darwin. He was hugely influential in shaping our understanding of biological evolution. I'd love to talk more about his work, including topics such as genetics, species diversity, and modern evolutionary studies.

Lastly, I would greatly benefit from the opportunity to learn about Hellen Keller's life. She managed to overcome obstacles that were even greater than those of most individuals and her spirit and perseverance have great value for me. Through dinner, I would be able to get to know her more, ask her questions about her experiences and maybe even learn that she might have some special missing pieces that could help me.

Altogether, I believe these four figures offer a mix of interesting perspectives on a wide range of topics, and I would have a very engaging and educational dinner conversation with them.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
For my own special dinner party, I would choose to have dinner with eight ancient Greek playwrights and thinkers: Solon, Pindar, Pericles, Sophocles, Euripides, Aristophanes, Socrates, and Plato.

The table would be set in the midst of a flowering garden, with white and gold floral arrangements, bronze statues of Greek heroes, and roman obsidian slabs for the table. The food would be Fitillia, Zithara Jam, Abelmosux, Pydra, and Mallosiou. The drinks would include retsina, thurmus, kykeon and krithomelious.

The evening would begin with Solon welcoming everyone to the dinner, lifting a kykeon toast to Pericles, who would then recite a soliloquy in which he explains his vision of the ideal Greek city-state. Pindar would weave in and out, taking sips of fitillia and reciting his famous odes with passion and wit. Socrates, with a large glass of thumus in one hand and a wreath of laurels in the other, would hold court with a series of riddles and paradoxes that left the guests both mesmerized and puzzled.

Euripides would recline in a nearby alcove, sipping on a mixture of mallosiou and thurmus, and drawing intricate illustrations in the wet sand with a stick. He would regale the guests with tales of his plays — Medea, Hippolytus, and the Bacchae — and share his thoughts on the nature of tragedy and human suffering.

Sophocles, with a wreath of ivy on his brow, would raise his voice in an epic soliloquy about Oedipus Rex and the implications of fate versus free will in our lives. Aristophanes, by contrast, would release a burst of riotous laughter, tossing his wreath of roses to the wind as he regaled the guests with the most absurd and farcical tales. Plato, sitting beside a quiet Socrates, would respond with a thoughtful silence, the twinkle in his eye betraying his admiration for the eccentric philosopher.

As the night wore on, Pericles would deliver an impromptu speech urging the scholars to renovate Athens, while Socrates would lambast every single person at the table for their flirtations with delusion. Euripides would continue to draw in the sand, growing increasingly complex with each illustration, while Aristophanes would pounce on every chance to poke fun at politicians, philosophers, and playwrights alike. Plato would nod thoughtfully, the laughter lifting him out of his reverie. Pindar, meanwhile, would hum a tune as he gathered the lanterns and engraved them with her name, the guests listening intently as he recited another ode by the light.

As the evening drew to a close, Solon would stand up one last time and give a speech of gratitude to all the guests. He would declare this gathering to be the most impressive assembly of minds he had ever seen, a testament to the enduring power of art, philosophy, and human intellect. And in his heart, he knew that the ideas spawned at this symposium would be remembered long into the future.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd love to have dinner with Aragorn, Gandalf, and Legolas.

Aragorn: Elendil's heir, tracker, fighter, and future king of Gondor.

Gandalf: Istar, scholar, and wizard with the wisdom to hide under deceit.

Legolas: Elf prince, marksman, and tutored strategist.

Conversation:

Aragorn: Shall we discuss the makeup of our armies?

Legolas: Of course, Aragorn, but remember our Elven archers can hide and strike.

Aragorn: Compliments always improve morale, Legolas; but the Uruk-hai threaten Rohan too.

Gandalf: Thalorim. Elrond had a guard ferried by mirror. For them there'd be no sea to part.

Legolas: Gandalf, you're a bringer of bad news don't you?

Aragorn: Not for Dean Ingold.

Gandalf: Your Elven counterparts would never have fallen to Sauron's ring if he wore it.

Legolas: We did not fall, Gandalf. Sauron corrupted the corrupted Hobbits.

Gandalf: Aye, but they wandered into the darkest valleys. We, convinced in our judgement, laid aside our battle wear for a moment of thought. 

Aragorn: And, Gandalf? You could've sent word to Meriadoc and Peregrin from the Moria gates, or Isildur's Bane from Lothlorien.

Gandalf: They must act of their own will. Though, fate has dragged them into all our battles.

Legolas: Aye, and our archers and I must provided them sanctuary for their exertions in protecting Gondor's borders.

Aragorn: And the Letters of Faramir. Why didn't we know more about Faramir?

Legolas: To have known might have cooled our boldness, Aragorn. We held onto the possibility.

Gandalf: The eagles finished the battle at dawn, Aragorn.

Aragorn: Must you keep pressing my buttons? Come Legolas and I'll mention the letters to our fathers and our mothers.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Aristotle, Socrates, Plato, Freud, Jung, Hitler, Kant, Darwin, Bush

Initial invitations all reject except Aristotle, Socrates, Plato, Freud, Jung, Kant, Darwin, Bush. Hitler says no, thanks, he plans to be at the theater this evening.

Everyone else arrives on time and makes small talk. Bush speaks only after being prompted. Bush is a quiet and respectful member of the dinner party.

When introductions are done, I start the first discussion:

"So Jung knew water was a healing element popular among the Mandeans in Mesopotamia who worshipped a god named Anahita. The Assyrian goddess Anahita has effigies depicting water flowing from her body, and this identificy of Anahita with water was later transmuted with the later goddess Ishtar. But Anthropos is a more scholarly reference and it includes references to the goddess afar as the Parthians. Jung includes many of these references in his 'Red Book'. He references this forgery in his 'Red Book' as well, which was his own metaphorical life journey. What are your thoughts on this?"

Freud chimes in: "Hermeneutics of misinterpretation – it is impossible to interpret anthropological evidence and apply a psychoanalytic lens without projecting bias. The reification of Anahita into a cosmic figure of water sustains the male ego, which has projected this image of itself which is then attributed to a female figure."

Bush speaks up: "Anahita was always the goddess of waters, of the heavenly bodies, and of weaving. The Mandeans revered her and still do. The holiest image of Anahita, located in the Ruzeh Monastery, depicts the divine feminine with streams of water pouring from her body." 

Kant nods. "Indeed, Kant believed knowledge arises from the accumulation of human experience, not from Divine revelation. Perhaps Jung projected his own ideas onto the anthropological evidence instead of observing it objectively. And these 'projections' may reflect his own bias towards the divine feminine."

Those thoughts prompt a discussion with Socrates about his insistence on pure definition. Socrates quotes: "If you know all the adjectives that apply to a rope, then you know everything that the rope is". This shifts the discussion towards pure logic, a theme insomnia of ancient Greece and Socratic dialectic.

Aristotle, Kant, and Plato engage in a discussion about the nature of causality while Jung and Freud separately muse about the collective unconscious and techniques of psychology. Darwin speaks about the role of natural selection in shaping the adaptability of the human psyche, while Bush states his opinion that religion plays a vital role in shaping society.

The dinner is an eclectic mix, with everyone bringing their unique background and expertise to the table. By the end of the night, the tables are turned. I am no longer the host but rather a fellow philosopher joining the group in their discussion.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei
Albert Einstein
Thomas Edison

Galilei Galileo was an Italian astronomer, physicist, and engineer who played a major role in the Scientific Revolution during the early modern period. He was one of the pioneers of modern experimental science after the scientific method had become established in physics by Galileo's contemporary Francis Bacon.

Albert Einstein was a theoretical physicist who developed the theory of relativity, one of the two pillars of modern physics. His work led to the creation of nuclear energy, and he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for his services to theoretical physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect.

Thomas Edison was an American inventor, scientist, and businessman who developed one of the first commercially practical incandescent light bulbs and founded General Electric. He was a prolific inventor, with over 1,000 patents to his name.

At the dinner table, we would discuss our scientific breakthroughs, our life experiences, and how we impacted society.

Galileo: "I've always been fascinated by the universe, and I've made some ground-breaking observations about it."

Einstein: "The nature of space and time has always been something I've been fascinated by. My work on the theory of relativity changed our understanding of those concepts forever."

Edison: "I've spent my life inventing things that make people's lives better. From the light bulb to the phonograph, I never stopped creating."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei
Isaac Newton
Albert Einstein

North Eastern Italian cuisine, smoky hummus, grilled lobster, red wine

Space, Time, and Reality (a game show)

Galileo: What an amazing experience. I've always been fascinated by how we can describe motion using mathematics? Do you think it will ever be possible to use mathematics to describe not just motion, but everything in nature?

Isaac: It's entirely possible, Galileo. Newton's laws of motion can predict the path of any object under the influence of a known force. And as for everything in nature, I believe that the laws that govern the universe are mathematical in nature. Put a few basic rules down mathematically and you've got the whole universe!

Albert: I agree with Isaac to an extent, but I have another idea. I believe that the nature of reality is much more complex. There are things that simply cannot be described using the rules of mathematics or physics as we currently understand them. I believe in things like alternate universes and consciousness as fundamental aspects of reality.

Isaac: Albert, your ideas may be on to something. While I agree that our laws of physics work incredibly well, I do feel that there is something more to reality that we haven't uncovered yet.

Galileo: It's incredible to think about! I can't wait to see where our science takes us next!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I chose to invite Leonardo da Vinci, P.T. Barnum, and Marie Curie for dinner.

I first met Leonardo da Vinci as I'm seated at the head of my long dining table, passionate in conversation with his friend and patron, King Francis I of France. I was delighted to overhear the two of them discussing Leonardo's plans for a flying machine. "I shall build it, come what may," he was saying. "And when I am dead, my work will continue as though I were alive."

Following an exchange with the King, I was then envited to sit with P.T. Barnum in a corner. We discussed his many amazing shows and inventions, including his famous hoaxes and world spectacles. P.T.Barnum was a great storyteller with an uncanny ability to mesmerize his audience. "The public," he said, "don't want to be told what to think, they want to be shown where to think." Those are timeless words that always resonate within me. 

After the dinner menu and drinks were discussed, I fetched Marie Curie. Once she arrived, we got stuck in that conversation together. She complained that when she began research, there was no institutional arrangement for women scientists. She also described the various difficulties she had endured along the way, but never underestimated the importance and spirit of her research and scientific integrity. This allowed her to achieve a great deal of recognition and tenderness, and laid the foundations for the radiation field.

As we finished dessert, we were joined by Leonardo da Vinci once more. He brought me along, as he took his friend King Francis I into the next room, striking up a conversation with a stranger. "Good evening, madame! Conosci la magia della chimica d'aria?" (Good evening, lady! Have you had news of the frescoes at the Chateau de Chambord? ")

Marie Curie introduced me personally to him. He was eager to hear about her work, and said that the greatest painters of his age palagitated the insight of the concept of X-rays. It was clear to me that Maris Curie had a particularly keen sense of humor. She whispered to me, "it is very difficult to be a scientist and a woman." Then she said, "maybe I have more things to teach them than they teach me," adding, she continued, "work, work, work."

Audible laughter could be heard around the table. We positively enjoyed our imperial feast.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Sure, here are some figures from history I would like to invite to my dinner party:

1. Helen Keller
2. Marie Curie
3. Albert Einstein
4. Plato
5. Winston Churchill

I would be so excited to converse with these five people. Helen Keller, an American author, political activist, and lecturer who was deafblind, was also famous for her endowed sense of humor and resilience. Her strength and determination are admirable and inspiring.

Marie Curie, a physicist and chemist who conducted pioneering research on radioactivity, is another lady admiring. She has won two Nobel prizes (for her contributions to the investigation of radioactivity, and to the genesis and propagation of X-rays). Her story of loving and supporting her partner Pierre and continuing his research on their own after his death is also something I would like to hear about.

Albert Einstein was also a great scientist and an iconic figure in physics. His theory of special relativity and his famous E=mc² formula helped the world explore more about time, space, and matter.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The dinner party is in my imagination and is being hosted for the following historical figures: Rabbi Adin Steinsaltz, Dalai Lama, Ibn Tufail, Sheikh al-Tantawi, and Albert Schweitzer.

My table guests have joined me for a special dinner to deliberate and debate Religion and Humanity. 

It all started when I, the Host of the dinner, invited the guests, and then prepared the menu. I ordered for some soft drinks, salads and pastries at a nearby high-end restaurant. The guests took the chairs and we started.

Ibn Tufail was one of the first to speak. 'In my book, Hayy ibn Yaqzan, I explored the philosophical questions in the Quran'

'Your vision was so ahead of its time', I said, 'did you think about the modern interpretations of your work.'

'I was often discouraged by the scholars of the time', Ibn Tufail shared. He looked to Dalai Lama for support 'I was fortunate to have a strong friend in the Dalai Lama.'

Rabbi Adin Steinsaltz was surprised a friend of Dalai Lama was there. The Rabbi looked around the table. 'It is a pleasure to meet familiar faces. I have corresponded and read the works of Sheikh al-Tantawi, Ibn Tufail and Albert Schweitzer. They introduce fascinating perspectives.'

'What was it that intrigued you about Haus einer Seele?' Dalai Lama asked Albert Schweitzer.

'Animal welfare and the importance of other humans' health' he replied, 'my experience in the jungle was challenging but, dear Dalai Lama, look at how successful the malaria clinic I established has been.'

'Owes us a debt of gratitude' Sheikh al-Tantawi said. 'We are lucky you came to Gabon.'

'On the contrary,' Albert Schweitzer said, 'we owe you something.'

Everyone enjoyed the dinner, and there was much food to go around. The discussion was enriching and the guests shared a deep sense of respect for their fellow humans and the philosophies they represented.

It was a wonderful evening, captured in my books and on my camera.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
It's a fine night for a dinner party with historical figures, so grab a napkin, tuéte, and I will explain the guest list.

Table of Contents:

1. Sir Isaac Newton – guests from the Scientific Revolution.  
2. Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley – guests from the Romantic Era.
3. Frederick Douglass – guests who fought for abolitionism.
4. Roman emperors – guests from antiquity.

---
Sir Isaac Newton – guests from the Scientific Revolution.

Seated at the first table, an air of professionalism and confidence dominates. Guests include Galileo, Johannes Kepler, Isaac Lee, and Robert Boyle.

Galileo was the first post-Renaissance scientist who visually observed and documented celestial motion. Johannes Kepler achieved clarity with ellipses in three laws of planetary motion. Isaac Lee was Newton's mentor. Robert Boyle was known for his systematic approach to science and the kinetic or corpuscular theory of gases.

Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley – guests from the Romantic Era.

At the second table, we meet Lord Byron, Percy Shelley, Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley, and Alphonse de Lamartine.

Lord Byron was Mary's romantic interest and扶贫y friend who lived troubled and scandalous life. De Lamartine admired French Resistance to Napoleon, which injected change into society. Mary Shelley poetically evoked the concept of creativity, Romantic ideals, and individual expression. Percy Shelley wrote sonnets infused with passion and emotion, questioning social structures and the frank needs of humanity in his works.

Frederick Douglass – guests who fought for abolitionism.

Frederick Douglass, John Harlan, William Myerd DHS, Henry Gerrit Goldsmith, and Charles Sumner sat at the third table. They were all abolitionists.

Frederick Douglass fought for the civil rights of Black Americans during the 19th century. They also lobbied against slavery and believed that all human races were created equal.

Romans emperors – guests from antiquity.

Mark Antony, Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Gaius Julius Caesar, Julius Caesar, and Tiberius Claudius made up the fourth table, which was reserved for Roman emperors.

Nero and Tiberius were the most problematic Roman emperors to their times. Nero did Pisonian conspiracy. He killed his family and burned down Rome. This was just another of his bloodthirsty actions. At last, he drank poison and died in the royal bedroom. Tiberius was not a good emperor either. He was very loving to his grandson Tibera and devoted himself to him. But when Tiberius turned old, he was attacked by soldiers and became disabled. Thereafter, the power of the Roman Empire gradually declined. This decline would eventually lead to a miraculous rise of Germanic tribes.

Charmed by the eras they hailed from, everyone agreed dinner was simply divine!

It's a dinner party! Celebrities of history are making arbitrarily arrive, and it's your job to make sure that everyone is seated correctly and has something to drink. You eyeball the figures that have appeared.

"Hey there Euler," you say while pouring him a glass of wine. "Over there, by Voltaire. You guys get along pretty well, don't you?"

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The dinner is set in spacious dining room that sits 20. The room has dark mahogany wood-paneled walls, landscaping paintings, engraved antique mirrors and a large fireplace surrounded by carved wooden mantel.

Stefan Zweig, my long-praised author and playwright, sits at the head of the long table, dressed in his black tuxedo, with oversized black wire-framed spectacles and ash grey hair. We sit, and he tells us about the French Revolution, and how it changed the political and cultural landscape of Europe.

Sigmund Freud, the founder of psychoanalysis, sits next to Zweig, dressed in his dark blue suit, wearing a white shirt and a silk tie with a throat lozenge. He then narrates his theories on the unconscious mind and psychosexual development. I listen to him intently as he talks about little Hans and his Oedipus complex.

Near Freud, in a silver GQ suit and shiny black dress shoes, sits Cordelia de Laine, a successful female detective in New York. She began working as a private investigator, auditing insurance claims, and stole the show. During the diner, she showed her skills in solving crimes and exposed the corruption as well. And today, she was interrogated by the police, was asked about the crime she was solving, and she smiled confidently answering all of their questions.

Under my scrutiny, Marie Curie stands in her simple dressing gown, wearing her hair tied back and a lab coat. She enlightens me with her theory of radioactivity, and we share a warm moment together. I admire her discovery of radium, consider her as one of the most famous female scientists in history.

Eleanor Roosevelt sits next to me. Wearing an elegant black dress and red stilettos, she discusses her roles as a diplomat, activist, and humanitarian. She's straightforward in her views, often leading the conversation with a question or startled remark, and she's unapologetic in her statements. She describes how she was treated as an outcast due to her mother's sudden death, and how she was raised by her strict father. Throughout the conversation, Eleanor Roosevelt's warmth and kindness shine through.

Under my close observation, Jack Kerouac, the famous American novelist and poet, stands in his writer capable leather jacket and jeans. He calmly shares his travels and mongrel philosophies. He explains his book "On the Road" to anyone that will listen. He tells us the story of Sal Paradise and Dean Moriarity in what he calls a "spontaneous prose" style. He welcomes all thoughts and questions from the guests, including me. Jack Kerouac is a storyteller par excellence, and he brings his stories to life with a passion that's infectious.

The conversation flows smoothly, and I find myself transported to different times and places with each person's story. It is truly an honor to be in the presence of these remarkable individuals.

The table is inscribed with beautiful calligraphy and lit with large, antique chandeliers. The walls are decorated with exquisite mosaics depicting nature and wildlife, while adorned in ornate portraits of historical figures. The floors are finished with polished mahogany, reflecting the glow of the candlelight.

The grandeur of the dining room, combined with the company of these extraordinary individuals, creates an atmosphere of warmth and connection. question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Here are five historical figures I would invite to my dinner party:

Socrates - the Father of Western Philosophy who pioneered modern scientific inquiry. His ideas about ethics and morality, and his method of questioning deeply-held beliefs have influenced Western thought for centuries.

Leonardo da Vinci - a polymath who achieved unparalleled success as an inventor, engineer, scientist, mathematician, architect, sculptor, musician, anatomist, botanist, geologist, cartographer, and painter. He is often described as the most diversely talented person ever to have lived.

Mahatma Gandhi - a spiritual and political leader who led India to independence and inspired movements for nonviolence, civil rights, and freedom across the world. He believed that all actions should be guided by the law of ahimsa (nonviolence, non-injury) and satya (truth).

Shirley Chisholm - the first African-American woman elected to the U.S. Congress and the first to run for President as a major-party candidate. She was an outspoken advocate for women's rights, black civil rights, and the rights of the poor.

Frederick Douglass - a leading abolitionist, orator, writer, and statesman. After escaping from slavery, he became a powerful voice for the rights of all people, black and white, and was a vigorous critic of the country's policies regarding race, democracy, religion, and reform. 

Together, these brilliant minds would bring diverse perspectives and fascinating conversation topics to my dinner table.

Certainly! My special dinner party would consist of Albert Einstein, Queen Elizabeth I, and Muhammad Ali. 

Albert Einstein is a profound physicist who was a groundbreaking researcher in the field of theoretical physics. His major contributions to science include his special and general theories of relativity and mass-energy equivalence, E=mc2.

Queen Elizabeth I ruled England for almost 45 years, from 1558 until her death in 1603. During her reign, Elizabeth I, also known as the Virgin Queen, established a strong, stable government and brought much-needed stability and prosperity to the country after a period of internal strife and civil war.

Muhammad Ali was one of the greatest professional boxers of all time. Born Cassius Marcellus Clay, he later changed his name to Muhammad Ali after converting to Islam. Ali was known not only for his fighting prowess but also for his outspoken views on social and political issues, including anti-war sentiment and civil rights advocacy.

At my dinner party, we would have the pleasure of engaging in thought-provoking discussions about our shared humanity, the achievements and challenges of our respective eras, and the timeless quest for knowledge and understanding.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
March 14th, 2021, I invited Marie Curie, Frederick Douglass, Pema Chödrön, Buckminster Fuller, Lao Tzu, Neymar, and Michelle Obama to my dinner party. They are my models who inspire me to be a better person. Their names are etched in history.

They were seated to both the left and right of me, very close, as we were sharing the main table in my living room.

Next to Marie was Pema Chödrön, on her other side, was Neymar. On my other side were Douglass, Fuller, Lao Tzu, Michelle Obama, and Curie.

The dinner started with mixed drinks.

Marie Curie raised her drink to her lips and looked at me. “Thank you for this thoughtful gathering,” she said, “but I don’t feel quite as if I really belong.”

I explained, “But you’ve made it possible for the rest of us to belong in your world of science.”

Theoki hit her cup. “Oh, Mery,” she shouted, “Shall we fill a glass with wine, to Marie’s health.” She also took the chance to wink at her.

Pema Chödrön smiled at me. She looked genuinely happy about the presence of all these figures in history. “This is literally life changing for me,” she said.

Frederick Douglass examined the wine bottle. “You know, I was born a slave,” he said, and paused, looking around, “very few of our kind get to sit down to a feast like this.”

Buckminster Fuller showed up, tall and powerful. “We must make the most of our time together,” he said. “We must discuss ways to better the world more deeply together, we have much to contribute to the conversation. Let’s not waste time drinking together.” He wore all white, and seemed impossibly calm.

Zhuangzi looked at the sky and sighed, “Ah! So dense and so mysterious! Its depth knows no limits. The Formless Distant! Paradise beyond all comparison! The Primal Pervasive! The Supreme Mystery!”

I understood his words no matter he said them in old Chinese.

Michelle Obama filled her glass and looked around the dinner table. “Thank you so much for inviting me to this celebration,” she said, “and for all my other friends who are here, it’s a really special day for me. When I was a little girl, my mother would sit me down at the kitchen table and talk about names of leaders and heroes we could look up to, emulate, and learn from.”

Neymar placed one of his hands on Fuller’s shoulder. “Let’s all raise our glasses,“ he said, “to this incredible gathering.”

I believe everyone drank that in silence and continued for the rest of the night with conversation.

6. Marie Antoinette - Queen of France during the French Revolution, known for her extravagant lifestyle and alleged extravagant spending.

7. Emily Dickinson - American poet who lived in seclusion for most of her life and produced nearly 1,800 poems during her lifetime.

8. Johann Sebastian Bach - German composer, pianist, organist, and conductor who is one of the most significant figures in Western classical music.

9. Ada Lovelace - English mathematician and widely regarded as the first computer programmer.

10. Karl Marx - German philosopher, economist, historian, sociologist, anthropologist, journalist, and revolutionary socialist, known for his criticism of capitalism and his ideas on social and economic change.

Discussion Topic: Impact of Personal Harsh Experiences on World Events and Sciences

As a big fan of Plato, it will be cool to talk to him in person. I will make sure to ask him if he really did write that Republic all by himself and how he thought his student Aristotle learned everything he knew about him. What an interesting contrast they must have had. 

I'll also want to talk to a few other philosophers and scholars from history such as Michel Foucault and David Hume. avant-garde philosophers of the modern age, I would like to hear them discuss Foucault's theories on power and Hume's views on the limits of reason and morality. 

Furthermore, I'm also super excited to bring along a bunch of modern-day personalities I admire. I'd love to sit down and talk with Angela Duckworth and discuss grit and success. It would also be cool to hear what Burton Malandro thinks about the nature of reality. 

On the whole, I'm really looking forward to this dinner party and talking to these interesting and thoughtful people.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei, Leonardo da Vinci, and Johannes Kepler

"Welcome, guests, to a dinner party of minds," I announced, gesturing around the room. On either side of the firepit sat Galileo Galilei and Johannes Kepler. Across from them, Leonardo da Vinci leaned forward enthusiastically.

Da Vinci's gaze shot between his two peers, his mouth quirking up in a grin. **"Johannes!"** he enthused, waving a hand. **"Your drawings of the heavens are marvelous!"**

Kepler nodded, sipping his wine. **"Thank you, Leonardo. Though I believe you have surpassed me in your skilful use of perspective."**

Galileo's eyes gleamed as if they danced with the stars. **"Indeed, Leonardo. And please, Johannes, I must know: was the Sun centered in your celestial tables?"**

Kepler laughed, a deep throaty chuckle. **"No doubt you would insist, Galileo Galilei."**

Da Vinci stared at the sky with a furrowed brow. **"I confess, I was unable to distill a fulfilled answer."**

Kepler leaned forward. **"This is where our ways diverge, Leonardo. From my early studies, I could tell our worlds were not as Ptolemy considered."**

Da Vinci nodded thoughtfully. **"Yes, I see it now. A sun captured as the center of all, the Earth and her companions in ceaseless embrace around him—"**

Galileo laughed. **"You echo me, Kepler!"**

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Douglas Adams, Richard Feynman, Arthur C. Clarke

Great scientists and science fiction authors! I hope you three enjoy this dinner party!

Okay, let's get started. Einstein, why don't you tell us what got you into physics in the first place?

[Pauses, then smiles] Well, it started as a simple thought experiment, you see. Imagine you are in a train moving at the speed of light, holding a mirror in one hand and a flashlight in the other. Light travels at the same speed no matter who measures it or where they are, but would the light from your flashlight somehow catch up with you on the reflected light from the mirror if you'd be moving at the speed of light?

That puzzled me for a very long time, and eventually led to the special theory of relativity. Just imagine, your time and space expanding along with you, matching the speed of light exactly!

[Richard Feynman whispers to Arthur C. Clarke] He actually postulated you can travel faster than the speed of light through a wormhole, AC! How cool is that?

[Clarke also whispers] Yeah, and using exotic matter as props! Just like in my books!

[Adams interjects] You guys realize I'm also here, yes?

[Laughs] Sorry, Doug, Arthur here and I were just talking about relativity and your books! They're so full of inspiring and innovative thoughts - it's amazing how some of your stories eerily came true.

[Feynman nods] We all owe you in one way or another. You grasped the possibility of far reaches in science before we actually could picture them!

[Clarke nods] We all try to grasp strange and wondrous things. It's the best part of being a human, isn't it?

[Einstein smiles] Indeed. It's all about imagination and mystery. That's what makes the science so much fun - we can always go further!

[Feynman nods] Yeah, and I just wish I could run a hypothesis by you, AC.

[Chuckles] What is it?

Feynman raises an eyebrow and decides to go off course. "So, in your books, you establish this Heisenberg-type first rule,
"Hey, kiddos! Don't listen to your teachers!" Do you think that still applies in the classroom?"

[Feynman chuckles] Yeah, and "Don't trust the math either!"

[Clarke laughs] He does have a point. But there's something to be said for being a mix of both! You know, questioning things and accepting the scientific method at the same time.

[Douglas smiles] Right, it's still all about finding balance and learning from historical moments.

[Einstein nods] A balance between questioning and acceptance, to achieve a deep understanding of nature. And that's what we're doing now - sharing and exchanging thoughts, finding a new balance in our midst.

[Everyone laughs]

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
As a notoriously reclusive person, I usually don’t enjoy social gatherings. But if I ever did have a dinner party, it would be with a unique set of guests:
Galileo Galilei,
― Charles Darwin,
Moonwalking Robot to deliver wine,
Chire Sheya,
― Charles Sharples,
President JFK, and
Sherlock Holmes

I picked them because:

Galileo Galilei, Charles Darwin, and Moonwalking Robot are the three people who really spoke to me and helped me develop a clear sense of philosophy and life goal. Galileo was a pioneer of modern science and a true free thinker. His ideas about the heliocentric model of the solar system and his arguments for empiricism and evidence-based reasoning continue to inspire me. Charles Darwin was a brilliant naturalist and observer who taught us so much about the evolution of life on our planet. And of course, the moonwalking robot I met at rokon conference.

Chire Sheya is my childhood friend. His bravery and selflessness are truly inspiring to me. He has never backed down from a challenge, no matter how big or scary. His willingness to stand up for others and fight for what is right is something that I hope to emulate.

Charles Sharples and President JFK are my mentors. Charles Sharples taught me how to be a good friend and a good colleague. He is always there for me when I need him, and he pushes me to do my best. He taught me important lessons about kindness, perseverance, and loyalty. President JFK taught me the importance of leadership and public service. His speeches inspire me to use my voice to make a positive impact in the world, and his actions show me the power of integrity and courage.

Sherlock Holmes is the greatest detective of all time and someone that I aspire to be like. His sharp intellect, investigative skills, and ability to solve even the most complex cases are truly remarkable. His methods and philosophies are something that I try to incorporate into my own life and work. I hope to be able to use my skills to help others and uncover the truth, just like him.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Democritus, Aristotle, Socrates, Heisenberg, Newton, Gauss, Kant, Locke, Avicenna, Zeno, Hilbert, and Descartes.

Since we are having a dinner party, I like to believe that everyone indulges in sort of a ceremonial group activity like "toastmasters." The purpose of this is to introduce everyone to each other and share a bit of our lives.

Once we begin introducing ourselves, we are all thrilled by our close friendship with Plato. Plato believes he has the ultimate happiness andvirtue to lead a good life. He is the great philosopher, and so his past is full of his wonderful dialogues meant to lead us closer to the truth. Aristotle proposes that social standing, intelligence, wealth, and education all contribute to a person's happiness.

Socrates' wisdom and life was always focused on seeking knowledge and understanding. He was a great example to us of living a good life of wisdom, bravery, justice, and moderation. When captured by the city of Athens, he had the opportunity to escape, but instead chose to drink hemlock rather than flee. It is a testament to his courage to stay true to himself, despite the threat of death. His legacy of wisdom and integrity inspire us all.

His student Plato thought he went too far with his teaching. Not only did his teachings arouse the guardly of the public, but they also annoyed her democratic leaders. Thereby, he established the Academy in Athens to pursue the truth about the forms. He uses the allegory of the cave to teach about the form of the good and the epistemology of the forms. Plato goes on to propose that humans must gain knowledge of the forms in order to live a good life. His philosophy of reason resonates with us all.

On the other hand, Aristotle defines the good life as eudaimonia, which is the highest point of happiness and virtue. He lived a life full of the pursuit of knowledge in science, ethics, art, rhetoric and other topics. However, Aristotle thinks that Plato's theory of the forms is complicated and unnecessary. He examines human nature and society and proposes a system of ethics based on virtuous behavior and practical wisdom.

As a physicist, Newton is considered one of the most influential scientists in history. Newton's description of the theory of gravity and his laws of motion have laid the foundation for classical mechanics, which remain accurate and useful to this day. His theories have been tested and verified many times throughout history. Newton was even the President of the Royal Society for the entire length of his lifetime. Though he contributed a lot to physics, all aspects of his researches are based on the faith in deity.

Descartes is the "father of modern philosophy". He claimed "cogito, ergo sum", which means "I think, therefore I am" and disproved the traditional perception of God through science. On the one hand, Descartes promotes modern science and development, on the other hand, he argues for the immortality of the soul. We can also feel his logic regarding "methodical doubt", which is putting doubt on everything and drawing inferences only after thorough investigation.

Unlike the other great minds, John Locke believes that there are no innate ideas. Instead, he thinks we are born with minds that can be written on like blank sheets of paper. Locke draws on the epistemology of empiricism and creates the theory of knowledge. He emphasized the importance of experience. Only after man experiences the real world, he can gain knowledge about the real world. This way, mankind can be in accordance with the nature and serve him.

Hilbert is a mathematician and philosopher, and his fundamental invention in history is the axioms of geometry. In his scheduled speech, Hilbert points out his view on the relation between mathematics and science. He argues that any astronomical theory can be verified by mathematics. Hilbert and Gauss have close friendship as they are both mathematician, their friendship shows the beauty of mathematics.

As a great philosopher and polymath, Avicenna worked on many subjects, such as philosophy, medicine, religioscience, poetry, psychology, mathematics, astronomy, chemistry, claustrophobics, and more. Avicenna is among the most influential thinkers in the Islamic Golden Age, and he proposes the theory of knowledge about intuitionism and reason. He puts forward that knowledge has two methods of obtaining: intuition (tutuitus) and demonstration (demonstratius). His thoughts shape the rational spirit of the Islamic civilization at that time.

Greek philosopher Zeno of Elea is famous for his set of paradoxes. However, like many other philosophers, Zeno thinks that only reason can lead humans to the ultimate truth. From his paradoxes, he aims to theoretically refute the notion of plurality and assert the monadism of reality. He also opposes Heraclitus' ideas because Heraclitus thinks that everything changes, while Zeno thinks nothing changes.

Based on the social contract, James Locke proposed that the government cannot do anything that is not agreed by the people. Therefore, people have an inviolable right to revolution, which is to overthrow the unjust government. He believes that the purpose of every government is to protect the safety of the people. His theories about government hinder the expansion of despotism, which creates a good environment for the political development of mankind. His theories also have a profound influence on American and French revolutions.

Emmanuel Kant severely criticizes previous rationalism and empiricism, and he holds the view that reason and experience are combined. He believes that people not only acquire knowledge through experience, but also the cognition of the world is constrained by the forms of human thought itself. His thoughts add a new development to the western philosophy system.

Heisenberg, the acknowledged father of quantum mechanics, disagrees with defining the trajectory and position of subatomic particles at the same time. Instead, he asserts that events are probabilities, and the exact state of a particle is only determined after observation. His groundbreaking idea makes us rethink the nature of reality.

As Galileo Galilei's student, Galileo Galilei's body got severely broken and his sight deteriorated, but he never gave up research. He insisted that the movables are not affected by friction. Newton's Discoursus Mathematicus Opticus, dealing with the nature of light and color, and Newton's Law of Gravitation were appreciated. We can clearly see the spirit and wisdom of these great scientists.

Moreover, we'll enjoy a great Banquet. Everyone is answering questions and making conversations, and the exchange of insights and beliefs between the guests is much more interesting than all the delicious dishes.

Finally, let's sing together to celebrate the joy of friendship and the beauty of thought!
