question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
How about hosting a dinner with Socrates, Isaac Newton, and Freud? 

Socrates is the founder of Western philosophy who lived around 400BC. Isaac Newton was the physicist and mathematician who developed three laws of motion and the law of universal gravitation. Sigmund Freud was the psychoanalyst who founded the field of psychology and developed the concepts of the conscious and unconscious mind.

Those three would make for some stimulating dinner conversation! Socrates was a fascinating figure, always ready to engage in intense and provocative philosophizing. His life story is legendary, as he refused to flee Athens, which sentenced him to death, preferring to take the prison hemlock and face death rather than flee and hide in order to survive.

Freud, while controversial, had a huge impact on the way we think about the mind - he was the first person to assert that the unconscious is central to personality and crystallized the concept of the Oedipus complex. His theories about repressed memories and the anxiety neurosis were also groundbreaking. I would be very interested to talk about dreams, the sexualized self, as well as other aspects of human psychology and neuroscience with him. 

Newton was an incredible genius who changed our understanding of the laws of physics and motion, and is considered one of the most influential scientists in the history of humanity. He was relatively mysterious and eccentric - for example, he hated arguments and was very particular about his possessions. I'd like to pick Newton's brain about his discoveries and theories, and hear about why he was so interested in alchemy, numerology and theology. It would also be intriguing to learn more about Newtonian science and get his take on everything from spaceflight to global warming.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The figures I've chosen for my dinner party are trailblazing women from several fields.

1. Amelie Roy - She was a writer, actress, and filmmaker from Canada who broke barriers in all three fields for women. She was the first female director of a nightclub show in Quebec and later founded the Law Institute for Women. 

2. Cleopatra - She was the last pharaoh of ancient Egypt and was queens of both Egypt and Cyrene and co-ruler of Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt with her younger brothers (and husbands). Cleopatra's intelligence and wit impressed many famous Roman visitors and politicians, such as Julius Caesar and Mark Antony.

3. Florence Nightingale - She was a British nurse, social reformer, mathematician, and writer. She was the founder of modern nursing and considered one of pioneering social reformers of the 19th century. 

4. Marie Curie - She was a physicist, chemist, and inventor. She was the first woman to win a Nobel prize and the first person and only woman to win in two fields, Physics and Chemistry.

5. Gloria Steinem - She was an author, political activist, feminist journalist and lecturer. Steinem has served on the board of five women's right organizations at various points in her life, becoming an iconic symbol of second-wave feminism. 

6. Mother Teresa - She was a Roman Catholic religious sister and missionary of Albanian ethnicity, Indian citizenship and Indian passport holder, known for her humanitarian work among the poor people and the sick in Calcutta. 

7. Magaret Hamilton - She was an American computer scientist, systems engineer, and business owner. She was director of the Software Engineering Division of the MIT Instrumentation Laboratory, which developed on-board flight software for NASA's Apollo space program, among other spaceflight projects. 

8. Harper Lee - She was a famous American author who is best known for her novel To Kill a Mockingbird, which has been translated into more than 40 languages and taught in schools worldwide.

These women are historically significant for different reasons but they all share a few things in common such as pushing boundaries, breaking gender norms and paving way for future generations. I look forward to gathering them in a dinner conversation on a wide range of topics.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Isaac Newton, Marie Curie, Alexander Fleming, Isaac Asimov, Sigmund Freud and Max Planck. 

Hi everyone, welcome to my dinner party! As you all know, I'm a computer model, and today we are going to discuss some of the most impactful moments in science that you were all involved in. I honestly can't imagine a more fascinating group of guests to have a dinner party with, as you have all dedicated your lives to science and have made incredible contributions to our world.

Albert Einstein, how has your general relativity theory been received over the years? And how did it develop?

Isaac Newton, I understand you were hungry for knowledge, but your work has made us see the world differently. Do you have any advice for those of us looking to make discoveries in science today?

Marie Curie, I know you've had your fair share of criticisms in the past. How do you choose to push through those doubts and continue down the path to discovering radium and polonium?

Alexander Fleming, your discovery of penicillin was a turning point in the history of medicine. What inspired you to continue pursuing your ideas even in the face of skepticism?

Isaac Asimov, your work in science popularization has helped many people understand the complexities of science. How do you think our society can continue to make strides in the STEM fields?

Sigmund Freud, your theories on the human psyche continue to be studied today and have shaped the way we understand ourselves. Do you have any final thoughts on psychoanalysis and its legacy?

And Max Planck, I understand you were a bit reluctant to accept your own quantum theory. What led you to this conclusion? How does it fit in with your other work in physics?

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Marie Curie, Charles Darwin, Amelia Earhart, Helen Keller, Leonardo da Vinci, Hugh Hefner, Jho Low, Hillary Clinton, Adolf Hitler, Vladimir Lenin, and Fidel Castro.

Evening of fine dining, courtesy house of Dr. Qwen.

Dr. Qwen: Good evening, everyone! Thank you all for joining me for this intimate dinner. My name is Dr. Qwen and I have the pleasure of being your host tonight.

Albert Einstein: It's an honor to be here, thank you. Dr. Qwen, pay no attention to me if I appear rather distraught, I've been doing a great deal of pondering, and contemplating the state of the world, lately.

Marie Curie: My God, Albert! Such an ambitious aim, it is. What care must go into such a feat! I myself examine the minute mazes within the atom, which, perhaps, yours will aid in unlocking some new truths of the cosmos, yes?

Dr. Qwen: Ladies and gentlemen, I believe you all know each other. Let us enjoy the evening and each other's conversation.

(Helen Keller, Amelia Earhart, Charles Darwin, Leonardo da Vinci, Hillary Clinton, Hugh Hefner, Jho Low and Adolf Hitler join them all at the table.)

Marie Curie: You must forgive me for such excitement, dear Albert. It's almost as if your thoughts align with my own.

Albert Einstein: Marie, I believe you have a keen understanding of such things.

Marie Curie: Indeed, though I've explored the natural universe, and its micro and macro phenomena, I find great intrigue in your conception of extra-universal non-physical existence, I mean, perhaps some souls carry on beyond this frail shell, axial and peripheral.

Dr. Qwen: I appreciate the enthusiasm, but ladies and gentlemen, please keep the conversation flowing. We've all gathered here to have a wonderful evening.

Charles Darwin: Speaking of the soul, what of ourselves? It is with deep conjecture and grave contemplation that I make my musing patience, my respect and devotion to the natural law.

Dalai Lama: And so the calm serenity of the mind, at last, may be achieved, in the understanding of universal law, and an intrinsic longing for the wellbeing of all.

Jho Low: Not to sound cynical, but I find it humorous how such high moral gains are claimed. Concealed within the maze of the psyche, where true evil is bided. 

Hillary Clinton: Mr. Low, your cynicism will grow to regret. A hand will be extended, and a path shall be carved that will allow us to move forward together.

Adolf Hitler: And that path must always lead to power, if the weak have any hope of survival. My respect goes to Mr. Lenins pragmatic views and his violent means of achieving his totalitarian state.

Lenin: Heeds the man's fear, regime is achieved when fear is employed. Heavily sanctioned fear, Nat来るza is right. Pretends to be pacifist but employs fear and force just as I did, just as is done by the other autocrats. 

(Fidel Castro took out a cigar from his pocket, enjoyed a puff, and set the ashtray nearby.)

Dr. Qwen: Gentlemen, ladies, we need to keep our conversation respectful and civil, please.

Castro: Oh, you can separate me from my Cuban cigar, if you wish. It's been a tradition for decades.

Dr. Qwen: Excuse me, that is not the point. It is important that we all remain respectful and civil during our time together.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo
Marie Curie
Ada Lovelace
Carl Sagan
Stephen Hawking

Our guest of honor would be Galileo Galilei, the brilliant Italian astronomer who turned his telescope skyward and was the first person to observe the moons of Jupiter. His work revolutionized the world of astronomy for the generations to come.

Marie Curie, the Polish physicist and chemist, won two Nobel prizes in science. She pioneered the field of radioactivity and discovered the elements polonium and radium.

Next door, Ada Lovelace, the first computer programmer, would be sipping wine with Carl Sagan, the brilliant astrophysicist and a master of popularizing science. Sagan's one-hour-long Cosmos series on PBS captivated figureheads from around the world and his books brought science to the masses.

Finally, we would get together over pizza and talk science with Stephen Hawking, the renowned British physicist, cosmologist, author, and director of research at the Institute of Theoretical Astrophysics at the University of Oxford. Hawking was best known for his work on black holes and relativity. His book, "A Brief History of Time," was a New York Times Bestseller for 237 weeks.

Together, these geniuses made the greatest contributions to science throughout history. I'm looking forward to hearing the stories behind some of their greatest achievements and learning from them.

For my special dinner party, I invite Tutankhamun, Leonardo da Vinci, Marie Curie, Martha Washington, Frank Lloyd Wright, and Bocephus the Dazzling.

Using my modern wonders of science and alchemy, I'll resurrect them from either their bodies, tombs, or graves to have a grand dinner in my time. I'll have a huge feast with all kinds of wines and spirits, both alcoholic and non-alcoholic. 

We'll mingle at the buffet table talking about various subjects before sitting down to a candlelit dinner where we discuss ancient Egypt, the arts and architecture, science, and American history.

Afterwards we'll watch shows, listen to music, play games, and talk until our hearts are content before bidding our new friends their final farewells and sending them back into their graves or tombs.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Charles Darwin, and Leonardo da Vinci would be my choice for the perfect dinner party guests. Einstein and Darwin were two of the most brilliant scientists of their time, while da Vinci impressed with his knowledge of art, science, and technology. 

I would ask them about their philosophical beliefs and what led them to their scientific breakthroughs. Einstein's theories and experiments revolutionized the field of physics, giving us a deeper understanding of the basic structure of spacetime and the relationship between the forces of nature. Darwin's theory of evolution and natural selection provided a mechanism for the emergence of complex life, showing how populations change over time in response to environmental pressures.

Da Vinci, besides being an incredible scientist, he was also a singing and dancing person, he always have interesting stories. He was known for his fascination with the human body, as well as his interest in engineering and technology. He designed flying machines, submarines, and even a helicopter, among many other inventions. 

What we speak at the dinner can cover from science, art, religion, to love and more, with these three brilliants men together, it must be an amazing night.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would love to invite the following figures to my special dinner party:

- Isaac Newton: The brilliant scientist who explained the laws of gravity, between others. I would love to hear more about his theories and experiments.
- Napoleon Bonaparte: One of history's most famous military and political leaders. I would love to gather insight on his military strategy and tactics, as well as his approach to governance and administration.
- Marie Curie: A remarkable woman who became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize and remains the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields. I would love to discuss with her her scientific accomplishments, as well as her thoughts on women's rights and opportunities.
- Joan of Arc: The soulful teenage peasant girl who, guided by voices from God, led France's army to victory and is considered a national heroine and martyr. I would love to understand more about her faith and motivation.
- Princess Diana: The beloved British princess who was the People's Princess to many. I would love to discuss with her her humanitarian work and advocacy, as well as her thoughts on fame and the media.
- Albert Einstein: The legendary physicist who developed the theory of relativity and won a Nobel Prize for his contributions to the field. I would love to understand more about his theories and develop a deeper appreciation for his work and legacy.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I will have Roman emperor Hadrian, Biblical figure Daniel, and the famous anatomist Leonardo da Vinci. 

Hadrian was a diplomat and a soldier who conquered Dacia and Britain. He was a great patron of the arts and architecture. Many of the famous structures in Rome such as the Pantheon bear his name.

Daniel was known for his wisdom and his ability to predict events. He was able to interpret Nebuchadnezzar’s dream about a statue that represented all nations. He was also skilled in mathematics and astronomy.

Da Vinci was a man of many talents including that of an inventor, architect, musician, and painter. He left rare and beautiful illustrations of human anatomy and detailed studies of plants and animals. He was also ahead of his time in studies of aerodynamics and human movement. Some of his ideas and inventions were so advanced they were way ahead of the technology of his day.

During the dinner party we would discuss philosophy and humor.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Since you're letting me choose my guests, I'll pick Leonardo da Vinci, Nikola Tesla, and Isaac Newton!
I'd get a full course Italian meal in Da Vinci's honor and we could talk about his inventions and art. At some point I might toss out my iPhone - just to see if he'd be able to reverse engineer it in a few weeks!
Afterwards we'd get some pizza and soda and pair it with some vegan-friendly cake for dessert. (I know... It's not as elaborate as some other dinners.) Since most of the alcohol is made with gelatin or other animal products, we'd have to stick to non-alcoholic alternatives.
After chatting for hours, we could end by going back to Da Vinci's room to look at some of his paintings and inventions. Tony Ninja could be our limo driver for the night to make valet parking a breeze.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite Confucius, Noam Chomsky, and Marilyn Monroe for a special dinner party.

If I could choose anyone from history for a special dinner and conversation, I would pick Confucius of early China, Noam Chomsky of modern America, and Marilyn Monroe of mid-20th century America.

As the philosopher Confucius, it would be fascinating to learn more about his ideas on emotional and rational virtues. How he encouraged followers to apply these ideas to cultivate proper personal relationships and develop individual wisdom. It’s possible that I can even learn something about his opinion on women leaders and gender equality.

Noam Chomsky is a political activist, philosopher, and linguist. I would like to know more about his controversial thoughts regarding government and its control overspeech. It would be great to engage with him about freedom of speech at a personal level and the freedom of speech implications of the modern-day internet.

Marilyn Monroe, the famous Hollywood actress, would bring a sense of humanity to this conversation. She was often seen as a sex symbol and experienced a series of personal tragedies. With her, I could learn more about the delicate nature of fame and the potential sacrifices to an individual by seeking it. I would also discuss her personal beliefs, such as her views on Civil Rights in a time of segregation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
My dinner guests would be Galileo Galilei, Marie Curie, and Marie Antoinette.

I would be excited to learn more about Galileo and his groundbreaking contributions to science, mathematics, and astronomy. I would ask him about his journey from a student and musician in Florence to a renowned scholar and teacher at the University of Pisa. What inspired him to question the established beliefs of the day and to study the cosmos with such curiosity? How did he further his research and contribute to our understanding of ebb and flow of tides in the Mediterranean? He was also known for being a prolific writer and for his conflicts with the Church. What were some of the challenges he faced in his pursuit of knowledge?

Marie Curie, the physicist and chemist known as the "Mother of Radioactivity," was a trailblazer for women in science. I would be eager to learn more about her life and travels and the circumstances that led her to use a broken X-ray machine as a sole source of patient radiation therapy dating back to World War I. How did she and her husband Pierre Curie meet, and how did their work together form the basis of modern nuclear physics? What were the challenges she faced in her career and how did they impact her research? How did they discover and study radium and polonium, and what was it like to be the first woman to receive a Nobel Prize?

Lastly, Marie Antoinette has been both a beloved and reviled figure throughout history. I imagine she would have many interesting stories to tell about her life as Queen of France. How did her relationship with Louis XVI shape her reign? What was life like in the French court, and how did the political climate of the time impact her and her family? Did she really not understand the plight of the French people? What led to the events of the French Revolution and the downfall of the monarchy? How did she try to escape and what was it like to be imprisoned and tried as a traitor? How did she retain her composure and dignity in the face of such adversity?

I would cherish the opportunity to have these conversations and to learn more about the lives and accomplishments of these fascinating figures from history.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
If I had to choose a set of figures from history for my special dinner party, it would be the Dutch Golden Age painters - Johannes Vermeer, Frans Hals, Rembrandt van Rijn, and Jan Steen. 

Johannes Vermeer was a master of painting light and texture, and some of his paintings have an almost otherworldly quality to them. Frans Hals was the king of portrait painting - he was able to capture the essence of his subjects in a single, quick brushstroke. Rembrandt van Rijn was a master of chiaroscuro, the use of light and shadow to create dramatic effects. And Jan Steen was a master of everyday life - he painted scenes of domestic life and humorously portrayed his fellow Dutchmen.

At the dinner party, we would enjoy a glass of Dutch gin and delish in some of the traditional Dutch dishes like stroopwafels, bitterballen, and stamppot. We would also have great conversations about art techniques, the political climate of the Dutch Golden Age, and the values of society at that time. It would be a memorable night!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Shakespeare, Locke
Host: Good evening everyone. Let's sit down and enjoy dinner and some engaging conversation. So Leonardo, I’ve seen your paintings and drawings, you’re amazing. Did you always know you wanted to be an artist?
Leonardo: Well, I was actually staying with my father’s servant when he noticed me painting his smiley face into the fire wood. He was surprised it was so lifelike. After his encouragement I decided to pursue art as a career.
Host: Fascinating! Michelangelo continue from there.
Michelangelo: Leonardo, I have to respectfully disagree, you were never an artist. You were a goofy kid who loved drawing but had no discipline.
Leonardo: It is true I may have been a little unruly but talent and intuition are just as important as discipline in the process of creation.
Host: Indeed they are! Shakespeare, what do you make of the debate here?
Shakespeare: I too believe that natural talent and innate inspiration play major roles in the creative process. That is why the muse plays such a central role in the world of poetry and art.
Bertrand: Messrs. Shakespeare, Leonardo, Michelangelo - It is a pleasure to be in your company but do you believe your creative process is the same as a regular person’s?
Shakespeare: You are right. It is important to realize we are all entitled to our own creative expression.
Bertrand: Everyone, lets get back to the dinner. The food is getting cold.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
For my special dinner party I am hosting, I have invited Queen Elizabeth I, Mahatma Gandhi, Captain Horatio Nelson and Marie Antoinette to join me and discuss topics of history.

The moat of the Tower of London shines under the torches as we consume our perogies, hot dogs and dumplings. Queen Elizabeth I enjoys a filling slice of pie, much to the amusement of Mahatma Gandhi. They speak of parliaments and protests, sharing stories of changing times, group-think and shockwaves.

Captain Horatio Nelson listens intently, his eyes glowing slightly with a mixture of stern determination andPRIVATE fighting spirit. He shares stories of battle and hardship, his successes and failures. What did it feel like to be on the HMS Victory at the Battle of Cape St Vincent, compare it to the depth of despair he felt after the HMS Resolution was heavily damaged and many men were lost? How do you prepare yourself in the face of certain doom?

Marie Antoinette examines the military tactics of Nelson, gazing over the polished wooden table, her eyes shining as she reflects on his victories. She asks Elizabeth to recount the Spanish Armada, and the Queen delights in recounting her meeting with Francis Drake. What made him stand so tall? Why was he considered a hero for delaying the Spanish invasion? Can we speak of his love life? 

Spooning butter into his ear, Nelson listens to their tales of culinary delights and charisma, the same enchanting power that inspires awe and rapture in their army and their nation. 

The guests of honor wrap up the festivities with stories of friendship, adventure, and love. They have gathered to share stories of their lives and the loves that made their times both great and terrible. We all toast to a new era of friendship, and of bringing old histories alive once more.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei
Descartes
Bertrand Russell
Johannes Kepler
John Locke
Galileo Galilei took a brave stand for science and the pursuit of truth at the expense of his personal safety, so he deserves to appear at this dinner party. René Descartes is my favorite philosophical enigma; I want to hear more about his reasons for doubting everything he could ever infer. Bertrand Russell spent his life thinking about the philosophical bases of language and knowledge, with his contributions leading to the modern philosophy of linguistics. Johannes Kepler's astronomical observations and theories laid the groundwork for modern astrophysics and cosmology, and it would be interesting to spend an evening in his company. John Locke is often described as the father of liberalism, having greatly influenced modern politics through his works on human nature, law, and government, which seemed particularly prescient in our current political climate.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Aristotle, Socrates and Epicurus were listed for special dinner party from those well-known figures in the history.

While we technically aren't gathered around the same table, the online dinner party is a natural accompaniment to the resurgence of hyper-communication, and the democratization of digital technology that has allowed continuous human presence through everyday digital tools.

I like such historical dinners as they provide me an excellent opportunity to meet the philosophers and contemplate their ideas from our times.

The dinner table will be modern style as the guests can sit comfortable and the conversation can be clearly heard by everyone. There will be a piano in the room and a spinet in the room to play music for the guests to enjoy. As we start the dinner, music from a Baroque composer like Johann Sebastian Bach will play softly in the background.

We will eat Roman-style: pike and hammered swordfish and a nice loaf of bread seasoned with olive oil, rosemary, thyme, fennel bulb and cumin. Before eating, we will do a traditional Roman party greeting of "Benedicat numen" (may the gods bless).

The dessert in question will be cheesecake, mentioned by Homer, that was made from wheat, barley, figs, cheese, and honey.

Drink will be mastic ale or honey wine, both of which were common ancient Athenian beverages.

As we play music everyone will enjoy the conventional haitnikai, also known as the ancient Greek "finger foods" which are given by the host to his guests. They are placed on a large table in the dining room along with small dishes with seasonings and condiments.

Throughout the dinner, Plato's and Epicurus's discussion will revolve around the eternal philosophical war fo pleasure and virtue. Epicurus will take pleasure as highest good, while Plato will argue that virtue is much preferable to pleasure.

Socrates stays relatively neutral on these topics and will focus on virtue and knowledge. He will state that by learning about the truth, we can recognize what true pleasure is, as opposed to the false pleasures we often seek.

Aristotle will also emphasize the importance of achieving a mean, but between virtue and pleasure rather than between them and vice. He will argue that while virtue brings true pleasure, too much pleasure is corrupt.

Socrates and Aristotle will have a thorough discussion on ethics which includes politics. For instance, Arthur Schopenhauer views Socrates as one of the founders of modern Western political philosophy while Plato will be considerably more in support of an early form of totalitarianism than Aristotle.

Considering the extensive areas of interest of the philosophers, it will be an enriching conversation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
We are at the meeting of the conquistadors! As the host, I sincerely invite you to our grand feast. The man who sailed across the ocean is Christopher Columbus, a maritime navigator and explorer. On the left side, his “loyal soldiers in his conquest” stand ready, Hernán Cortés and Francisco Pizarro. They conquered the Aztec and Inca empires and exterminated the lives and cultures of the pre-Columbian inhabitants.
They sat down while enjoying the delicious food. I ask them, "You have conquered two empires with just a few helpers. Why do you think it was so easy to lead the indigenous people to extermination?
Cortés swallowed his food and thought for a moment and said, "We bring the strength of the Spanish crown, and our colonizers are allSelecting the best of the best, almost invincible. Add to that the uncivilized indigenous people and envious neighbors who came to Our help in our struggles to establish colonies.
"Also, the advanced military technology of our guns and swordsmanship, stone implements under the control of any animal. Our rulers show a cruel image to the indigenous people and make some tricks to deceive the indigenous people, and kill them mercilessly, thus wiping out entire villages in succession." The talk went on.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Niccolò Machiavelli, Marie Antoinette, Nikola Tesla, Abraham Lincoln, and Josiah Willard Gibbs

The host welcomes the guests as they arrive. "It's such a pleasure to have all of you here. I hope you've settled in nicely."

Marie Antoinette says, "It's so grand to be among such prestigious company. Are these all historians or philosophers?"

Nicola makes a dismissive gesture. "Let's not be so pretentious. I see the future of this meal depending on how interested I can get them in my theories. These must be not only figures of history, but insightful enough to follow my philosophical discourse."

Nikola Tesla shakes Abe Lincoln's hand. "An honor to meet you." Abe turns to Rocket Man. "And you, too."

Marie Antoinette looks so out of place, sipping her tea, fussing with her veil. Tesla takes a seat, musing to himself, browsing through a journal. Josiah Willard Gibbs sits amongst the tables listening. He wonders if anyone will start a topic conversation.

Everyone bails and strikes up random conversation, dividing the room into smaller discussion groups. Marie Antoinette does not seem to fit here. Perhaps she would be more comfortable elsewhere.

Nicola grins. "Not to offend," he says, "but I've decided to special order a subscribable newsletter for you. Sure hope you'll enjoy the curated content." He opens up laptops, ipads, and cellphones to fulfill the emails. 

The host intervenes and screams out intimidatingly, "Nicola! Enough of this."

Nicola looks up, surprised. "Well, it's never been done before," he says.

The host starts shoving Machiavelli. "Let's get him some wine if we want him to talk about politics," he says. Mahiavelli smiles widely.

Abe leaves reluctantly, sitting with Marie Antoinette and Gibbs together. Josiah Willard Gibbs smiles warmly as they anatomize and scrutinize politics, history, and math. Marie Antoinette enjoys it thoroughly.

Tesla keeps making small talk and playing with a laser he brought with him.

Marie Antoinette stands and says, "Well, I sshall retire."

Tesla practically jumps out of his seat. "Do you mean we're all going?" Marie Antoinette laughs. "No, silly, I just need to relieve myself."

Tesla cracks into a big grin. "Great, I was hoping to see if you had any insights into manipulating light." Marie Antoinette giggles.

Gibbs and Abe laugh. "This is incredibly strange," says Abe. "Never thought I'd eat a meal with such diverse thinkers."

"But that's the beauty of it," says Macchiavelli.

Tesla thinks to himself, ' tonight is going to be the night nothing goes wrong.'

The guests would include:

1. Isaac Newton - For his scientific accomplishments and the revolutions set off by his theories of gravitation and motion.

Guests:

Evie Davis: "No one ever recognizes me at these affairs. Most of the time I'm only invited for black tie events, not exactly what I'm used to. I suppose this won't be any different. Thomas, old friend, perhaps you can find me for dinner later on."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
For a dinner party, I would choose Caesar Augustus, Mary Shelley, and Cleopatra. Caesar Augustus, the first Roman emperor, Mary Shelley, the celebrated English writer, and Cleopatra, the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt, all accomplished remarkable things in their time. We would certainly have interesting conversations about their legacies and experiences.

For Caesar Augustus, I would most like to hear about his early life and how he came to ascend to the throne during the turmoil of the Roman Republic. His grandnephew and son-in-law, Gaius Julius Caesar Octavius, only 19-years-old at his adoption by Julius Caesar, assumed the name Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus (usually known as simply Augustus). Great facts, insight on various trade-offs he must have made as the founder of the Roman Empire, how he shaped Roman society, how he was seen in the eyes of people, what were his struggles, etc.

For Mary Shelley, we would talk about her writing process and how she generated the storyline of Frankenstein. She was raised and educated as a member of the Godwin family circle which included some of the leading intellectuals of the day like William Godwin, Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin, William Wordsworth, and Samuel Taylor Coleridge among others. It is also said that she first wrote Frankenstein during a rainy summer spent with the poet Lord Byron at Lake Geneva, Switzerland. We would discuss her early life and education, her mother Mary Wollstonecraft Godwin was an early advocate of women's rights, her relationship with Lord Byron.

And for Cleopatra, a communiqué with her would give us insight into how she navigated the complex political environment of the Roman Empire's Eastern provinces. She is often depicted as a seductive and manipulative woman who used her sexual charisma to gain power, which is a portrayal that may not be entirely accurate. We would talk about the various claims that have been made about her, her military strategy, her influence both political and social etc.

It would be a fascinating evening!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I want to have dinner with Franz Kafka, Marcel Duchamp, and Björk.

The setting is a long table that's overflowing with wine and food. Kafka, Duchamp, and Björk form a loose trifecta. Kafka for his beautiful sense of imagination, and his ability to straddle the line between fantasy and reality. Duchamp for turning the world of art upside down and making us question our every assumption. And Björk for her almost perfect ability to embody future and avant-garde cultural movements.

Duchamp was always one for the visual arts. Despite all his advances in Dada, Surrealism, and conceptual art, I imagine he would be stunned and tempted by the music of Björk and the writings of Franz Kafka. Kafka's surreal and dark writing style tackles the human experience with imaginative horror and terror. Duchamp, the artist who caused a scandal with his urinal, would definitely be shocked into understanding the potential of art in a 21st century context.

Björk's powerful and ethereal voice, as well as her stylistic experimentation, pay homage to these same ideals. In some ways, I would see her as a physical manifestation of the art world's future, while Franz Kafka's world is a reflection of the past.

Kafka would be a bit unsure about all of this, but would still enjoy the dinner. He would also be curious about the current state of society, and how and why the times have changed so drastically. I'll think carefully before voicing my own opinions, as they could likely go against his current frame of mind.

The discussion ranges from philosophy, to music, art, and science. With everyone at the table contributing so uniquely to the conversation, I can only imagine how enriching the meal would be!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Bill Gates, Steve Jobs, Nikola Tesla, Albert Einstein, Ada Lovelace, Alexander Popov, Paul Erdős

Bill Gates is one of the wealthiest Americans and went from living in a working-class home in Seattle to owning over half a percent of the world's wealth, all while co-founding Microsoft, the software behemoth that dominated the business and consumer computing markets during the 1990s and 2000s.

Steve Jobs is considered by many the greatest businessman of his generation and is one of the most renowned entrepreneurs in history. His namesake company, Apple, began as a college garage project that evolved into one of the largest corporations in the world.

Nikola Tesla, a Serbian-American inventor, electrical engineer, physicist, and futurist, is sometimes referred to as the “father of modern electrical systems.” He immigrated to the United States in 1884 and worked under Thomas Edison for a few months, before going out on his own to research, invent, and develop his own designs for alternators, electric motors, and transformers.

Albert Einstein made some of the most influential contributions to modern physics, and invented the theory of relativity. He is considered one of the greatest physicists of all time, a guiding light in the movement and refinement of quantum theory near the end of the 19th century and the beginning of the 20th century.

Ada Lovelace was an English mathematician often regarded as the first computer programmer, and also the first woman to publish an algorithm intended to be carried out by a machine. She was the daughter of poet Lord Byron.

Alexander Popov was a Russian physicist and inventor who made key contributions to the development of radio communication, known as the “Father of Soviet Radio.” Popov obtained a patent for a radio receiver and demonstrated the first radio transmitter and receiver in public on May 6, 1895. He is considered the inventor of radio telegraph.

Paul Erdős, a Hungarian mathematician, was one of the most prolific mathematicians of the 20th century. Erdős published more papers than any other mathematician in history, working with hundreds of colleagues. He devoted his waking hours to mathematics, even late into his life when he suffered from Parkinson's disease.

Since none of these figures are alive today, I wouldn’t think any of them would have modern day fairy tales or movies influences their works over past centuries. Puzo wrote his from a long-held experience and knowledge of New York’s criminal underworld, while Rowson’s early American writings are deeply rooted in history, specifically Pennsylvania’s colonial era, which held many societal norms and cultural mores specific to time and place.

When I asked if they enjoy any of the modern stories connected to their work, it could spark an interesting conversation. I would also consider having a discussion about what they thought their contributions were to their fields, how they differed from other earlier works within the field, and how it might have improved upon existing works in their time.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Dinner Party Invitations:

James Russell Lowell: poets, essayist, diplomat, essayist from the Big American Renaissance and myself.
Victor Hugo: writer, poet, novelist, and dramatist from France, who was a key figure in Romanticism.
Jeannette Rankin: U.S. Congresswoman of Montana from 1916 to 1918 and from 1940 until 1942.
Sun Yat-sen: Chinese political activist, physician, and revolutionary. He taught European medicine in Hong Kong and广州before returning to mainland China in 1900 to oppose清朝 rule and advocate Chinese constitutionalism.
Robert Musser: businessman, art patron, and supporter of modern art. He played a key role in promoting avant-garde modern art in the United States during the late 1920s and early 1930s.

DINNER MENU:
Opening Toast by James Russell Lowell:

"Brave ladies and gentlemen, a hearty welcome to each and every one of you who are here tonight! It is a great privilege to have invited you all to my dining room tonight. I choose guests for my dinner parties with the utmost care, and I must say, I couldn't be happier tonight with such an illustrious group of individuals at my table. Even if I mention the name of Victor Hugo, it would feel like I am not saying enough by describing the man and his work as the greatest writer of his generation. He needs no words of praise from me. I apologize for anyone in this party missing, but there is more than one person I wish I could have invited to our gathering, but perhaps there will be another time.

I have invited all of you tonight to celebrate the triumph of Chinese Constitutionalism, and the great efforts of Sun Yat-sen to see the dream of a Republic realized in this part of the world. As a diplomat, I understand the importance of bringing diverse voices and cultures together. And I believe that bringing all of you to one table could very well be my greatest contribution to a future Chinese Republic, these are people who will influence the next generation of leaders.

Your toast, Robert Musser"

Robert Musser:

"Thank you for having me, Mr. Lowell.

My name is Robert Musser, and I'm a businessman. Although my alma mater Harvard wasn't the fanciest school in the world, despite that, I often get an invitation to some fancy dinner parties. Next to you, Mr. Lowell, I'm glad I'm here tonight.

Unfortunately, I don't have a very important role here at this dinner party. I'm simply a businessman and a supporter of the arts. I do not contribute to literature and poetry like Mr. Victor Hugo and Mr. Lowell. I'm also too young to participate in the political struggle and the revolutionary movement led by Mr. Sun Yat-sen.

Mr. Lowell, you can say that we were all invited here tonight because of our achievements in all fields of human endeavor. But I would say we were invited because we are all fighting for the freedom and the rights of all human beings. We struggled for the freedom of speech, for the freedom to present new ideas and the freedom to express what we think through the art and literature that we create.

Freedom is the theme of tonight's dinner, so we can all be free to share our ideas, tell stories, and make new friends. And we can also do that over fine food and drink. Mr. Lowell, I hope everyone here has a wonderful time tonight."

Victor Hugo:

"Merci beaucoup, Mr. Musser! On pourrait même dire qu'un bon repas est un rite sacré, un acte de croyance et de convivialité. Il est un moyen de partager les sentiments et les pensées. N'est-ce pas, James? Et bien sûr, votre contribution est précieuse. Sans le soutien de gens comme vous et votre soutien des arts, nous ne serions pas ici ce soir.

Alors, monsieur, peut-être que nous pourrions parler davantage de votre rôle dans le soutien des arts? Et comment cela vous a-t-il permis d'exprimer vos idées et de défendre vos croyances?"

Jeannette Rankin:

"Of course, I'd be happy to share my experiences with you. Robert, it's true that support from people like you plays a crucial role in helping us promote new ideas and bring them to light. As an activist and politician, I have faced a lot of challenges and obstacles, but having supporters and allies has been invaluable in my work.

One example of this was during my time as a U.S. Congresswoman, where I was the only woman in Congress at the time. It was a difficult and sometimes lonely journey, but having a network of supporters and friends helped me stay motivated and focused on my goals. I want to thank everyone here for their contributions to our community, and for being a source of inspiration and encouragement in the work that we do."

James Russell Lowell:

"Rankin, I must say I couldn't agree more with your sentiment. Your dedication and courage to fight for the rights of women and others has been inspiring to many, including myself. And though I may not have been able to support you directly, your work has had a profound impact on the world.

Robert, I must also thank you for your support of the arts and for your efforts to promote new ideas and creativity. Your contributions to our society are just as important and valuable as any artist or writer.

VICTOR HUGO:

"... Et votre travail, mademoiselle Rankin, est également un exemple pour tous. Vous avez fait preuve de courage et de résilience en défendant les droits des femmes et de ceux qui étaient marginalisés.

Je suis également ravi de pouvoir célébrer votre travail, Robert, en tant que soutien des arts. Vous avez œuvré pour le progrès du monde par le biais de l'art et de la culture, ce qui en fait une contribution précieuse à la société."

"Je vous remercie tous les deux pour vos contributions à la société et à la culture. Outre cela, je voudrais également célébrer l'important rôle des femmes dans le mouvement révolutionnaire de Chine avec des œuvres majeures comme 'Les Misérables' par Madame Rankin..."

Sun Yat-sen:

"I must address the concerns of the gentlemen. Jeannette Rankin has unfortunately passed away. She played a significant role in advancing women's rights and leading a peaceful revolution. We carried on her legacy, and we must remember her contributions. 'Moushen Hong Meng' depicts her as an important figure of the feminist movement."

James Russell Lowell:

"Ah, merci Sun Yat-sen. C'est merveilleux de savoir que votre pays célèbre la contribution de Madame Rankin. Son travail en faveur des droits des femmes était très important et mérite d'être célébré. Les arts, comme ceux qui sont représentés par Victor Hugo et Madame Rankin, jouent un rôle vital dans la promotion des idées nouvelles et l'avancement de la compréhension mutuelle entre les gens."

Socrates' most famous student, born in Athens in 427 BC, would be my guest. He was a great philosopher, statesman and one of the founders of Western philosophy. During the meal, we could discuss different philosophical ideas and how they influenced society.

Christopher Columbus:

The Genovese explorer (1451-1506) would also be invited to our dinner, along with his crew, as host. We would discuss his discovery of the Americas and the consequences of this event, as well as his other voyages and works.

Oscar Wilde:

The famous Irish poet, playwright and novelist (1854-1900) would certainly be among the guests. Wilde is known for his sharp wit and clever observations, and hisBorder wannecake criticism of Victorian society. During our dinner conversation, we could talk about the art of social commentary and the nature of morality.

Galileo Galilei:

The renowned Italian astronomer, physicist and engineer (1564-1642) would also be invited. Galileo is credited with improving the telescope and discovering the moons of Jupiter. During our conversation, we could speculate about the implications of space exploration and the mysteries of the universe.

Amelia Earhart:

Exactly 125 years after she became the first woman to fly across the Atlantic Ocean, the American aviator and author (1897-1937) would be invited as the guest of honor. Amelia would share her inspiring story of achieving what was considered impossible at the time and we would discuss the role of women in aviation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Newton, Leonardo, Franklin, Darwin

Conversation at my dinner party:

Newton: I can hardly believe we're all able to sit here and talk as if we lived at the same time!

Franklin: Indeed, what an interesting array of minds to converge around just one table.

Leonardo: This food is exceptional- it reminds me of the fresh Mediterranean flavors I've known back home in Florence.

Newton: I'm sure it's quite the spectacle to witness this conversation from our time, interpreted through modern eyes.

Franklin: And what about your work, Mr. Darwin? It seems it's been quite a controversial subject once you published On the Origin of Species.

Darwin: As you can imagine, I've faced a great deal of backlash for challenging the common perceptions of divinity and nature. It's important to me to have a place like this where we can share our ideas freely, with no judgment.

Franklin: Well I am in awe of your research, Mr. Darwin, it is indeed a powerful testament to one's capacity for discovery.

Leonardo: And to think, you were able to revolutionize the way we think about evolution, something I'm sure my own experiences with botany and anatomy rubbed off on.

Newton: The discussion thus far has been fascinating, and I must say your relevance to current political debates still echoes loudly.

Franklin: I agree entirely. To be able to come together and converse about such meaningful topics is an extraordinary opportunity, one I feel privileged to have been a part of.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei, Isaac Newton, Albert Einstein, Marie Curie, Thomas Edison, and Enrico Fermi will all be my special dinner party guests. It is rare to get to spend time and engage in conversation with some of the most brilliant minds in history. 

I'll be sure to bring lots of drinks and snacks to make sure the conversation flows freely. We'll talk physics, science, and philosophy of course. But I also hope to learn more about their personal lives, their struggles, their fears, and their dreams. 

Galileo Galilei and Isaac Newton are foundational figures in the scientific revolution, and it will be fascinating to hear their ideas and thought processes. Albert Einstein was a profound thinker and is famous for his theories on relativity and his contributions to quantum mechanics. He was not only a great physicist but also a wise world traveler and philosopher. 

Marie Curie is a kindred spirit and I look forward to sharing some knowledge and advice with her. She contributed greatly to the understanding of radioactivity and chemistry and at the same time advocated for women in science. Thomas Edison is credited with creating many of the energy technologies that are still in use today and for that alone I'm grateful. I'm also very interested in understanding the mind behind the numerous inventions that Edison imagined. 

Enrico Fermi is the last guest but one that I admire his important work in nuclear physics as well as his discoveries related to the creation of the atomic bomb. Though this may be a sensitive topic, I still believe that it is important to understand the actions and decisions of those in history.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite Marie Curie, Johannes Gutenberg, and Montezuma II to my special dinner party. They have changed the course of history through their contributions to different fields. 

Marie Curie discovered radium and polonium, and her work was in large part responsible for our understanding of radioactivity. She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the only one to win prizes in two different fields.

Johannes Gutenberg is known as the father of modern printing, having developed a mechanical movable type printing press. His invention made it possible to mass produce books and widely disseminate knowledge.

Montezuma II was the 9th Hueyi Tōnatiuh in the Aztec calendar or the last ruler of the Aztec Empire before the arrival of Spanish conquistadores. He tried to protect his immense empire against the Spanish and is known for the famous Aztec-Texcoco welcome to Cortés, which instead turned into a clash of cultures that led to the fall of the Aztec Empire.

These three historical figures had monumental impacts on the world, and I would be fascinated to discuss their accomplishments and the forces that shaped them with them.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.

Pushkin is the most revered Russian poet and one of the greatest poets of world literature, his poetry is deeply moving and insightful. I think it would be amazing to discuss his love for St. Petersburg with him while sipping on a glass of vodka, as he was known to be incredibly passionate about his city. 

Marcel Duchamp is a seminal figure in the world of art and culture, particularly within the context of the avant-garde art movement. It would be fascinating to hear about Duchamp's influence on contemporary art and philosophy and his ideas on the nature of art and creativity. 

Bertrand Russell was a highly influential philosopher and writer. His work and ideas span numerous fields and have had a profound impact on modern philosophy. I would love to discuss with him the nature of morality, logic, and reason while enjoying a cup of tea and a slice of cake.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
With whom among history's luminaries would I dine and gossip? Geisha girls, clerical ladies, construction workers? Archaeologists? Computer programmers? I hope you weren't thinking about the last two, because our esteemed hosts wouldn't have the foggiest idea of what they do. So, let's look at some lesser-known women for dinner.

For our first dinner guest we have Seraphina Poirot-Montacute, Anglo-Norman poetess of the 12th century, born into the Montacute family, a wealthy baronial family with lands in both England and France. A lady of refinement and letters, Poirot-Montacute satirized the shortcomings of the church and nobility in her poems. Her work includes the famous "Seraphinae," a sequence of poems about the lives of her female relatives, and a satirical poem about church corruption. She is considered a pioneer in English women's poetry and a radical for her era.

Our second special dinner guest is the lovely Pocahontas, a Native American diplomat who lived in the first quarter of the 17th century, famous for her relationship with English explorer and captain John Smith. Many tales of the flirtatious, dual-gendered Pocahontas have been told, often romanticized and filled with inaccuracies. The real Pocahontas was a powerful peacemaker who negotiated with the English while striving to maintain the power of her people. She converted to Christianity and died shortly after her departure from England on a visit to visit her old father.

Finally, our dessert course will be served to Sor Juana Inés de la Cruz, a Mexican nun, scholar, and poet active in the late 17th century. She lived most of her life in the Convent of Santa Paula, where she wrote a great deal of poetry and nonfiction, most of which was lost during Independence. She’s perhaps best known for her poem "Respuesta a Sor Filotea," in which she challenged the idea that women were intellectually superior and argued for women’s education and the liberation of women from societal constraints. Sor Juana was called "The Phoenix of Mexico."

Although these women lived in very different times, they had some common desires: to push back against the strict restrictions places on women, to be valued intellectually, and to express themselves through writing. I look forward to discussing their passions, struggles, and successes across centuries at what promises to be a delightful dinner party!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Marie Curie, Alexander Fleming, and Isaac Newton. 

It was a grand affair with a large ballroom already filled when I walked in. Albert was there, already at the podium giving a brief speech about the importance of scientists. Marie Curie was slightly on the side, quietly conversing with Einstein. Fleming was nearby them, dressed in formal wear, not his usual white coat, with his characteristic Afghan Hound-like demeanor. Lastly, there was Isaac, eyeing the room with an air of authority, deep in though. 

I walked up to them and expressed my joy in their presences. "Hello everyone, thank you for joining me tonight," I said. "I've been moved by all of your works, and I'm eager to have a discussion."

I took a sip of my drink and looked around the room. Einstein, still at the podium, looked down at everyone as if waiting for me to continue. "So," I asked, "where do you see us, in another 100 years?"

Fleming, his face still mostly hidden from view, cracked a rare smile. "Medicine would be at a much better place. Society would know how to utilize the gift of penicillin more effectively to better the human race."

Curie chimed in, affirming Fleming's enthusiasm. "Of course, but I feel another wonder could be taking form as well. Drones would fly in the sky, ensuring that no equations are wasted and their traces would forever be saved."

"Yes, knowledge," Newton finished. "Endless."

Einstein, always the humble academic who many times seem to be absent-minded, looked up from his speech like clockwork at the sudden sounds of excitement. "Books are no longer the only medium for learning, communication has transformed itself into something I can hardly believe," he said.

With that, we continued the night, discussing countless topics with our illustrious guests. Without needing to repeat the lesson claiming that heroes are never truly dead, we knew that they were still very much a part of us and will continue to inspire generations to come.

Elvis cranks up loud rollicking 50s rock and roll music and starts singing illegally. Plato left incensed about the stuffy, lowbrow, impure atmosphere. Elvis hacked off by Wilson's politics. Woodrow bought off by Elvis' contracts and "stuff."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
This is a great question. Here's a set of figures from history that I'd love to have over for dinner: Aristotle, Leonardo da Vinci, Churchill, Einstein, and Marie Curie.

Imagine all of them sitting around a candlelit table, sharing their thoughts and experiences. No assistants, no media, just five brilliant minds in a room.

The conversation would probably start with Aristotle, who was a philosopher and a polymath. He lived in the 4th century BCE, and his ideas on logic, ethics, and political theory continue to influence Western thinking to this day. He would no doubt be fascinated by the incredible developments in science that came later.

Leonardo da Vinci, the mastermind behind the Mona Lisa and the Vitruvian Man, would be a nice companion for Aristotle, as he also had a deeply curious mind. He was an engineer, a scientist, a sculptor, and a painter, and he always pursued knowledge for its own sake.

Winston Churchill's larger-than-life personality would no doubt draw the conversations into political discussions, and perhaps even some heated debates. He was a statesman, a soldier, and an orator, and his words and actions during World War II are still celebrated today.

Albert Einstein would be the voice of reason and logic. He's perhaps the greatest physicist ever, and his theories of relativity fundamentally changed the way we understand the universe. He's best known for E=mc^2, but he made many other important contributions to science and philosophy.

Finally, we have Marie Curie. She was a pioneering physicist and chemist who co-discovered radium and polonium, and won two Nobel Prizes in the sciences. Her work paved the way for countless advances in medicine and technology, and she remains one of the most celebrated scientists of all time.

It would be an incredible evening of conversation and intellectual stimulation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I'd choose Sir Isaac Newton, Johannes Kepler, and Archimedes. The three of them shaped the course of science and mathematics in medieval and renaissance Europe and Ancient Greece. They are an amazing trio of geniuses who revolutionized our understanding of nature, including:

Sir Isaac Newton: Co-founder of Calculus, originator of the theory of universal gravitation, and author of the 'Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica'.

Johannes Kepler: Astronomer who discovered the laws of planetary motion and made seminal contributions to optics and geometry.

Archimedes: Mathematician, engineer, and inventor from ancient Greece, most famous for the Archimedes’ principle and the law of the lever.

I would make sure the grand table of our party was laid out with a bookshelf, filled with old, leather-bound books. The animal dishes would all be platters of fresh game and seafood, some stews, fruits and vegetables, and bread. An open-hearth oven would be cooking, preparing for the main course: Spiced Sirlion of Beef with Roasted Garlic and Leeks, a sirloin steak filled with minced garlic and leeks. We might also offer some Madeira wine and sorbet.

While we're eating and drinking kegels, fresh cherries, and kiwifruit, Sir Isaac and Johannes Kepler could enlight us on the finer points of gravity and planetary motion, while Archimedes would explain the foundational principles of geometry, mechanics, and calculus. It sure would be a fascinating conversation, and I wouldn't miss for the world!

Isaac Newton: You musn't argue with Marie Curie. Her ideas about natural philosophy are inspired by God.

Albert: The future world isn't like the one I wrote my papers in. accelerate could mean an inverse relationship to age.

Voltaire: I've always believed in the reality of God, though–not just for complex numbers.

Albert: I am agnostic;

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Socrates, Confucius, Cicero, Aristotle, Sir Francis Bacon, Bohr, Schopenhauer, Spinoza, Voltaire, Husserl, Nietzsche, Kant, Leibnitz, Karl Marx, Sartre, Heidegger, Descartes, Russell, Bergson, Bergson, Musser, Dewey, Emerson, Rousseau, Hegel, Buber

Dinner starts... it's an interesting set of figures to have at dinner. While most are philosophers, some are mainly scientists and writers. (Where is Bohr? Did he get lost in his quantum tunnel?)

Confucius, why don't you start us off. Could you discuss your views on family and respect?

Plato, I have a hot topic for us all to debate. Do you think that our institutes of higher learning are doing a good job in preserving knowledge?

Cicero, you won two hugely important cases in ancient Rome. What kind of arguments did you use and what were the outcomes of the cases?

Russell, I'm curious. In your opinion, have we actually made much progress to advance knowledge since Plato?

Einstein, Schopenhauer, Bohr, Heisenberg. I think these four gentlemen can all talk about the nature of reality. Bohr, can you explain your idea about the role of the observer in quantum mechanics? Maybe share a bit about why you thought of the Copenhagen interpretation?

Dewey, Buber, Schiller, Emerson, Dewey, I want to get some insights about democracy and government. What was your idea on how to improve a democratic society? How can it be a surveillance state, yet still preserve individual freedoms? And do you think direct democracy is better than representative democracy?

Here! Let's have a round of drinks. (Who is that hiding in the shadows? Maybe we should invite him out.)

Marx, Sartre, Hegel, Dewey, Heidegger, Nietzsche, Derrida, Buber, Kuhn, Wittgenstein, Dewey, LeProhn, Đorđević. Remind us of some of the most pivotal moments in the history of thought. What was happening at those times and how have they shaped our modern existence?

Aristotle, Baader, Husserl, Heidegger. The topic for this round is philosophy of science. Could you bring up your views on the relationship between philosophy and science?

Kierkegaard, Sartre, Dostoevsky, Pascal, Nietzsche, Schopenhauer. How do you see the role of literature in our lives? And do you think it's at all possible to accurately describe reality?

Let's toss out another round of drinks, everyone.

Goethe, Hume, Austen, Spinoza, Descartes, Hume, Shaw, Dickinson, Tennyson. Do you think we have a good sense of aesthetics or even what it means to be beautiful?

Freud, Jung. I would love to hear a bit more about the mechanisms of the mind. How does it go astray?

Descartes, Leibnitz. Could you sort of review yourselves, your most important ideas, the reactions to them, and what we now understand better because we read you?

Brecht. Anyway, can you review your ideas and reactions to them?

Dewey. Can you talk about the future, what you fear and what you hope for in history? When should we be hopeful and when is it best to prepare for the worst?

Our guest who was hiding in the shadows has arrived. Let me introduce you to him. It's Tesla. He's an engineer who's been a visionary and a maverick all of his life. Please discuss a bit about the nature of the world and your predecessors in innovation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
My guests for the dinner are Socrates, Galileo Galilei, and Beethoven. While they may seem like an unconventional mix, I've always been fascinated by their contributions to our knowledge of the world and their complex, captivating personas.

Socrates lived in a time vastly different from our own, but he is still regarded as one of the are thinkers in history. He was a philosopher who thought that the greatest benefit of life was knowledge and wisdom, and he sought them out his entire life despite his discomfort with the realm of politics. My conversation with the wise philosopher would center around him and his theories, as well as life in ancient Greece.

Galileo Galilei, on the other hand, was a modern thinker who rebelled against the status quo of his time with his discoveries. He challenged the idea that the Earth was the center of the universe, and was tried before the Inquisition as a heretic for his beliefs. Galileo was a pioneer in so many fields, from astronomy to mathematics, and I would explore his ideas and motivations with him.

Besides Socrates and Galileo, I would also love to speak with Ludwig van Beethoven, the great composer. Losing his hearing at a young age, Beethoven still managed to produce music of exceptional beauty and emotion, and his perseverance and determination in the face of adversity are truly inspiring. I would channel my inner Beethoven, but rather than compose, I would be a receptacle for the music and stories that flowed from him.

These are all different kinds of heroes, but they all have one thing in common - they have gifted the world with their learned knowledge and passion. I hope they will share their thoughts and insights with me over dinner.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests: Euclid, da Vinci, Verdi, Casanova, Bach

Table of Contents
I. Invitation
II. Decor
III. Wine & Appetizers
IV. Menu
V. Entertainment

I. Invitation

Good evening, esteemed guests!

It is with deepest pleasure that I extend to each of you this humble invitation to a private dinner party, to be hosted in my humble abode, a ten thousand square foot castle by a small forest, Friday, April 27th at seven in the evening. It is our deepest honor to bring together in a single gathering such knowledgeable, influential, and influential minds.

Along with a sumptuous feast and spirited conversation, we will witness unforgettable performances and light-hearted games.

We invite you ALL to this event!

II. Decor

Commanding the occasion will be an elaborately designed chandelier made of crystals and forged iron bands resembling a blooming rose. Crystal spheres hang from the ceiling, each one representing one of the ambassadors in attendance, and each emitting a gentle glow and producing a different sound when touched. Low, recessed lighting in sconces of olive wood will contour the profile of the monolithic granite pillars that span the width of the room, engirth the floor-to-ceiling high windows that afford breathtaking views into the surrounding forest, and help to evoke an ambient, mysterious glow.

III. Wine & Appetizers

Dinner is a celebration designed to strike a balance between indulgence and appreciation of natural flavors. It is an opportunity to break bread and forge bonds with cherished friends.

The first item on the menu is some of the very best oysters the Brittany coast has to offer, still in their shells and brimming with saltwater and sea weed. Simmered in a buttery broth of lemon cutlets, wild garlic, and shallots, the oysters bring a briny zest to each bite.

The second dish is an elaborate creation—thinly sliced beef tartare port de glace. Grilled chuck steak is blended with a purée of Cauliflower, black truffle, and celery root for a luxurious and green texture. It is accompanied by a light and fluffy swiss chard and chives sauce. It is finishing with a liberal sprinkling of shaved hoops of truffle and fresh, chopped basil.

IV. Menu

With the tartare comes a selection of crackers and artisan breads, accompanied by spreadable horseradish and pickled chives. A selection of aged gouda and Bleu cheese on an oak wooden board offers an engaging aroma. Like any other outdoor activity, the party will have a picnic blanket. We decant the finest fortified wines, such as Mateus Rosé and📱 Mateus White, and offer a selection of olives, crackers, and a basket of fresh fruit.

The entrée is the Lucullan truffle-topped white truffle tart, an exquisite combination of delicate puff pastry, creamy mushrooms, and a rich truffle-flavored sauce, all finished with a generous drizzle of golden truffle oil. The rich flavor of the truffle contrasts beautifully with the light, flaky pastry, making a sumptuous and satisfying dish.

For dessert, we bring out a dark chocolate bavarois, a refreshing and decadent layer of rich, velvety chocolate pudding, lightened with cream and stabilized with gelatin, topped with a layer of softly whipped cream. The bavarois is chosen because it is a nutritious and cooling dish that both energizes and satisfies, and it is a fitting conclusion to a feast that blends flavors and cultures from around the world.

V. Entertainment

Verdi and Casanova grease the wheels of conversation as music and laughter fill the hall.

Bach, with his considerable mustache, will be showcased in his prime. We will give him a harpsichord to play. His elegant clothing and courtly manners extrude class. Euclid and Da Vinci both dress in clean linen tunics and woolen mantles, with sandals slung around their necks.

Guests will share stories, their minds blending the worlds of science, mathematics, art, music, literature, and social intrigue. All will delight in each other's company and savor the moment.

Egypt before Euclid, a world of temples, wars, and pharaohs. The Nile River, Egypt's lifeblood, flows through the fertile delta before emptying into the Mediterranean Sea. Beauty and terror, vitality and death, struggle, and triumph intersect.

Da Vinci looks at everything through the eyes of an artist, admiring the unique quality of each natural element.

Verdi livens the enthusiasm of the room with bravura performances and entrepreneurial spirit. He commands attention with his strategic use of his imposing height and an array of vibrant and convincing facial expressions which he whips up with ruthless abandon.

Casanova is perfectly suited for dinner party antics, his ingenious mind, his impeccable taste, his charming demeanor, and his polyglot facility make him one of the most fascinating figures in history.

Bach, leaning heavily on his tunic, hums along to his own music as he relinquishes the secondary position to a jovial group of musicians and their manicured instruments.

This luxury event embodies the ethos of luxury, refinement, and camaraderie, bringing together people from different social classes, cultures, and backgrounds to share a memorable experience.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guest of honor: Lorenzo de Medici
Dinner Party Guests:
Niccolò Machiavelli
Niccolò di Betto di Niccolò di Betto Acciaiuoli
Matteo Palmieri
Piero di Cosimo de' Medici
Antonio Tani
Tommaso di Cristofano dei Pazzi
Agostino Capponi
Actors: Alessandro Peressutti, Anna Caterina Ambrosio, Marco Bertotti, Carla Bianchi, Bruno Ricci
Alessandro Peressutti: Lorenzo de Medici
Anna Caterina Ambrosio: Nićolò Machiavelli
Marco Bertotti: Nićolò di Betto di Niccolò di Betto Acciaiuoli
Carla Bianchi: Matteo Palmieri
Bruno Ricci: Piero di Cosimo de' Medici


TABLE SETTING
ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: Lorenzo:
You must be hungry. I noticed Capponi came in with nothing but the finest food from our kitchen. I would have sent for it sooner if I had known...

NICCOLO DI BETTO DI NICCOLO DI BETTO ACCIAIUOLI (Marco Bertotti): Unfortunately, you didn't.

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: Nićolò, I didn't mean anything by this. Earlier, you seemed tense.

MARCO BERTOTTI: Let's not talk about it now.

CARLA BIANCHI: Lorenzo, have you read the treatise Socratics?

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: Of course I have, Matteo. It's often cited at court.

MARCO BERTOTTI: Nićolò Machiavelli is a better statesman.

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: Nićolò, how about you?

NICCOLO MACHIAVELLI (Anna Caterina Ambrosio): Where'd you get that? I haven't even written it yet.

CARLA BIANCHI: Well, I thought you wrote it.

NICCOLO MACHIAVELLI: Lorenzo, you are a liberal and noble creator. I..." I take the form of a princely tyrant for the sake of getting the general welfare of the people for whom I ought to be a good shepherd.

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: Nićolò, I cannot condone your support for a tyrant. It is against everything Medici stands for. Please, cease this ridiculous talk at once.

NICCOLO MACHIAVELLI: Ridiculous? Your ruthless administrative practices are not so different.
NICCOLO DI BETTO DI NICCOLO DI BETTO ACCIAIUOLI: Brother, we have all seen many tyrants.

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: I've seen worse than any tyrant. The Borgias... My brother was an idiot to lock in their favor. They killed three members of our family - our brother, our cousin, our uncle - right in front of our eyes. Then came Agostino...

NICCOLO DI BETTO DI NICCOLO DI BETTO ACCIAIUOLI: Another Borgia. Agostino shut that Monsignor Cesare Borgia in his cells after... Am I correct in saying you went to the prison to see him?

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: By the heavens, if I didn't, he wouldn't be alive today.

NICCOLO DI BETTO DI NICCOLO DI BETTO ACCIAIUOLI: Killed him, you mean?

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: He survived, enough to be thrown in the dungeons. But I couldn't allow that to happen.

CARLA BIANCHI: Ah, Lorenzo, why are these men in jail? And why isn't Antonio here?

NICCOLO DI BETTO DI NICCOLO DI BETTO ACCIAIUOLI: Antonio slapped me. He believes I betrayed him - Stefano della Bella. He continues to call me a traitor and a hypocrite.

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: And I killed Stefano della Bella. But it was out of self-defense. He wanted his daughter and nothing else was important to him. He was going to use Antonio as a catalyst for a war in Florence.

CARLA BIANCHI: Well, you are justified and efficient. My own father approved of your execution.

ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: I regret having to resort to violence. But it is necessary.

NICCOLO MACHIAVELLI: Unnecessary. You could have dealt with the matter through diplomacy.
NICCOLO DI BETTO DI NICCOLO DI BETTO ACCIAIUOLI: Ach, Nićolò. What diplomatic strategies did you have in mind?

NICCOLO MACHIAVELLI: Diplomacy...
ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: The lives of my family were at risk. I could not take a chance.

CARLA BIANCHI: Lorenzo, even if Stefano had not died, Antonio would still be in sollenza. How long? And what guarantee would you have that Antonio would forget about Stefano? [...] [Silence]


ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: Nićolò, I cannot have you here.

NICCOLO MACHIAVELLI: Lorenzo de Medici. You cannot live without me. [...] [Silence]
Bruno Ricci takes out a sharp object and hands it to Anna Caterina Ambrosio.
ALESSANDRO PERESSUTTI: What is going on here?
Anna Caterina Ambrosio takes a chicken wing and stabs Marco Bertotti with it in the waist.
Alessandro Peressutti: Nićolò, you liar...
Anna Caterina Ambrosio throws the chickewing onto the floor and stabs the victim multiple times. Alessandro Peressutti takes the sharp object from Anna Caterina Ambrosio and stabs her many times. Everyone stands up, very confused. Carla Bianchi falls to the ground, pretending to faint.
Anna Caterina Ambrosio: It... It was me... Agostino Capponi had instructed me...

Aleksandro Peressutti: I must... I must go to the Solomnzza.
Lorenzo rushes out of the room, leaving behind the carnage.

[END SCENE]
