When the night ends, you feel grateful to have met such incredible people from history in person.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
My Choice for a Dinner Party Invite List:

- Marquis de Condorcet
- Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley
- Immanuel Kant
- Napoleon Bonaparte
- William Gladstone
- Karl Marx
- Mahatma Gandhi
- Marie Curie
- Leon Trotsky
- Bertrand Russell

When I returned from picking up everyone from their respective points of detention, I was flabbergasted by the scene I was greeted with. One had somehow barricaded the entrance and another silently SETTINGS up the centerpiece while the remaining crew goaded the stragglers into position. As I slowly regained my bearings, I must say it did make for an intriguing start to the meal.

Bertrand Russell's sarcastic quip about the menu helped energize the room as folks found their seats. Leon Trotsky was point blank at him about his wooden spoon's purpose but Bill Gladstone coolly intervened with the comment, "To slow your eating, you Red file drawer!" I laughed at his out-of-character "arrh!" which brought a grin to everyone's face.

As the initial round went down, it was fascinating to watch Kant spar with Marx and find their shared agreement about conflict and oppression. He then turned to Rousseau for a flight on the merits of Salons. Some of the chatter made my head whirl, but who are we to complain about dinner table gab!

But, the showstopper of the night was when Gandhi created a tablecloth out of his clothes. Marie Curie! Challenge accepted! She proceeded to shrink the table using her radium, then I had to pull William Gladstone's legs out from beneath him to keep him from jumping screaming at the ceiling.

As a nod to our guests, I even passed out the indecipherable manifestos of both Marx and Trotsky to condense those pesky cloudy concepts behind Leftism into something palatable. The party was a smashing success and a lovely glimpse into history.

Enjoy the dining with a unique cast of characters!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would love to serve dinner with Euclid, Pythagoras, Archimedes, and John von Neumann

Euclid was, in fact, one of the greatest minds of the history. He taught mathematics in Alexandria, Egypt. He wrote thirteen volumes of "Principles of Geometry," which is a textbook of geometry still used today.

Pythagoras was not only a mathematician, but also a philosopher and musician. His best known declaration, the Pythagorean Theorem has amazed people for thousands of years.

Archimedes is a great mathematician and physicist. He was the first to measure pi and calculated the volume and surface area of a sphere. He also discovered the formula for calculating the volume of water displaced by submerged objects.

John von Neumann is one of the most influential mathematicians of the 20th century. He is the father of game theory, and his work significantly influenced the development of quantum mechanics and the structure of nuclear weapons. He was also a pioneer in the development of computer science, having built one of the first computers, the EDVAC. 

I can't wait to have an intellectual discussion with all of these people on the mathematics, physics, geometry and scores of other topics.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would have Sir Isaac Newton, Ludwig Boltzmann, Galileo Galilei, Albert Einstein, and Johannes Kepler at my dinner party.

I would start by thanking them for their contributions to humanity. Sir Isaac Newton developed the theory of gravity and laid the foundation for modern physics. Ludwig Boltzmann helped develop statistical mechanics and thermal physics. Galileo Galilei popularized the telescope and laid the groundwork for modern science. Albert Einstein developed the theory of relativity and a mass-energy equivalence formula. Johannes Kepler's work on planetary motion was crucial in the development of modern astronomy.

After a hearty meal and glasses of wine, I would speak with them about their work and lives. I would ask Sir Isaac Newton what inspired his theories of gravity and motion. I would discuss light with Galileo, since his studies of the sun helped us understand it better. I would quiz Albert Einstein on the details of his relativity theories, and also ask him for his thoughts on the prospects for time travel. I would ask Ludwig Boltzmann about the difficulties he faced in defending his ideas about entropy and the second law of thermodynamics, and how he overcame them. I would ask Johannes Kepler why he believed that planets moved in ellipses and how he arrived at that theory.

The night would be an unforgettable entertainment and discussion of some of the most important discoveries in science history.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests: Leonardo da Vinci, Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, Charles Darwin

Dish: umami udon noodles

Talk: We've been葾meeting recently for dinner and have enjoyed swapping theories and ideas. 

Leonardo, who better to unpack the natural world than you and Mozart the construction and evolution of music? But I'm still amazed at your peers trying to hold onto the flat earth theory at the time that you were designing both flying machines and a manual on the human body. I've always wanted to ask you questions about how you incorporated knowledge of anatomy into your drawings, like the Vitruvian Man.

Mozart, our brains produce the exact same sound waves, and your music often reflects philosophical concepts you've observed. I really admire how respectful you were to your patrons and mentors by working just within the scope they set forth, judging your own work by high standards.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Einstein, Picasso, Freud, and Ghandi.

Einstein was a genius physicist who revolutionized our understanding of the universe. He was also a pacifist and upfront about his beliefs, which sometimes got him into trouble. Picasso was an innovative painter and sculptor, known for co-founding cubism. He was also a prolific writer and intellectual, always finding new ways to express himself. Freud was the founder of psychoanalysis. He had a unique ability to treat mental health issues that others had abandoned, and his theories continue to influence psychology to this day. Ghandi was a revolutionary leader in India's fight for independence, who practiced nonviolent protest and influenced civil rights leaders like Martin Luther King Jr.

The four of them would make an incredible dinner party. Einstein could discuss his theories with Freud, wondering about the mind's role in our perception of the universe. Picasso might bring up new ideas that he has just been thinking about, and Ghandi could remind them all of the importance of using our intellect for more than just academics and art. I'd love to be a part of such a stimulating conversation.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
If I could have any dinner guests, I would like to have hosted Marie Antoinette, Florence Nightingale, and Lu-Tze.

Marie Antoinette is a name that rings out throughout history. Though we tend to think of her as the "Queen of France" who didn't careless of the commoners' plight, she's also known to have been quite liberal on philosophy, sciences, and women's rights.

Florence Nightingale is an inspiration to healthcare professionals all around the world. For her contributions to the nursing profession and her dedication to patients, she was nicknamed "The Lady with the Lamp." Her patients' well-being were her top priority - even more so than her own. 

Lu-Tze was the third Lung T'u, and a follower of Confucianism who lived during the time of the Warring States. Lu-Tze and his wife Ching Shih ruled a pirate confederation that started in the southern coast of China and later became one of the most powerful in Chinese history. He used his writing and his policies to keep his confederation strong and to protect his crews, and later wrote but never published a book titled "The Four Treasury."

It would be a lively conversation, I'm sure.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests: Marie Curie, Isaac Newton, Leonardo da Vinci, Aristotle, Eugene O’Neil, Jane Austen, Leo Tolstoy, and Albert Einstein.

Setting: My own laboratory/apartment, up with a video projector and an old-fashioned, messy dining table.

Host Speech: "Welcome everyone! Thanks for stopping by for a little dinner party here. Rarely do we all get to sit down at the same table to discuss thoughts, ideas, and curiosities. So let's get started!" 

Dinner Conversation:

Me: "What's something you studied/researched/created that you are most proud of?"

Marie Curie: "My discovery of radium and polonium, and my work on radioactivity, of course. It's definitely my most notable achievement."

Isaac Newton: "I suppose it would be my three laws of motion, and maybe the reflecting telescope. They are pretty separate from each other, but they were incredibly influencing discoveries, at least in science."

Leonardo da Vinci: "My inventions and art are probably what I'm most proud of. The secret to keeping my mind sharp was never stopping with a single vision."

Aristotle: "My Mentoring of students. While my ideas and work will always be with us, I believe the most important thing I did was help the next generation grow."

Eugene O’Neil: "I helped spark one of the most profound artistic revolutions to date. We may have our differences personally, but I am incredibly proud to have started and led the modern American stage."

Jane Austen: "My novels. I wanted to portray the world to a generation of America shallow girls who were content with platitudinous nonsense. I hope I did that."

Leo Tolstoy: "My novels 'War and Peace' and 'Anna Karenina'. Life and history were very present in my work, and I hope that theme allowed readers to learn their lessons from my work."

Albert Einstein: "relativity! I definitely want my work to always be remembered!"

Me: "What advice do you have for your peers of the future?"

Marie Curie: "Never let discouragement or intimidation stop you from following your passion."

Isaac Newton: "Study hard and work on truly understanding the subject. I stress it firmly that understanding takes patience and trial and error, but science is an amazing and beautiful thing."

Leonardo da Vinci: "Always be hitting the books. I am never going to stop learning because the world and its many subtle, fascinating mechanisms have no end to them."

Aristotle: "Blocks the path to progress."

Eugene O’Neil: "Inspire change through your art."

Jane Austen: "Never change your writing to fit societal norms, even if you think it might help you get published. Write the truth."

Leo Tolstoy: "I can tell you what I continue to learn: Use your journalism to inspire those around you. Promote peace and understanding."

Albert Einstein: "Never be afraid to explore. Failure makes the explorer stronger and better at the next task."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:

Welcome to my dinner party, historians. I've been so excited to meet each and every one of you!

Alexander, I've always admired your military genius and your sheer obsession with conquest. I couldn't help but notice the mixture of admiration and betrayal in your soldiers' eyes right before your death- what went on there? Caesar, I've really enjoyed reading your Histories. They're a lot more engaging than political mumbo-jumbo or ramblings about myself, if I say so. 

Galileo, your theories about the Sun at the center of everything just don't seem to sit right with me but I respect your contributions and brave stand in the face of religious politics. Eleanor, the fact that you used your influence to put an end to discrimination no matter the source is truly inspiring although it's difficult to understand how things like the cold war came to be.

Frederick Douglass challenged the harmony of my views on the human race from the moment I met you. Your eloquent speech and fierce resolve never fail to shock and motivate me. Leonardo, I believe you were unique and ahead of your time but at the same time I can't deny the influences your pieces have seemed to have on later works of art. 

Tesla, I'd love to talk to you about energy distribution and your failed attempts at imposing them. I'll ask more about your life in a more appropriate setting of course. 

Thank you all for honoring me by dining with me here and now.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Socrates, Plato, Aristotle

Overview:
The dinner party brings together three of the most influential philosophers in history from ancient Greece. They exchanged ideas and shaped Western thought, influencing countless generations to come.

Dinner:

(Conversation between the guests)

Socrates: Plato, I must say, your academy looks magnificent!

Plato: Thank you, Socrates, I spent all my money to build it. Not for any one individual, but for the benefit of all mankind.

Aristotle: I see what you're driving at, my friend. Harmonization of the interests of society as opposed to individual ones leads to true happiness.

Socrates: You express what many of us believe, Aristotle. But I do not understand why you do not join our small group of philosophers. At least once a week, we meet for dinner to discuss the meaning of life.

Plato: My dear Socrates, you were right about your discussion. However, I cannot join this informal group. My students would not learn anything if I were to absolutely disengage from them.

Aristotle: Plato, I must agree with Socrates, you definitely need to engage more with them. I fear your students are falling behind in their studies without the guidance of such a wise teacher.

Discussion during Dinner

(Conversation with the host)

Me: Aristotle, so many interesting talks. Plato, your written works are amazing, they provided a lot of fun for my students and I.

Plato: Thank you, my friend. People don't yet appreciate everything hidden between the lines of my writings.

Me: And don't worry, Aristotle, soon enough your works on ethics will be highly sought after. Where do you find inspiration for them?

Aristotle: Firstly, I have ain't disappointed by this great dinner party, lots of knowledge-swapping and constant movement of thoughts. Moreover, I have always sort of an interest in observing people and society which were great inspirations of my philosophical works.

Me: Great! It sounds like you’re leading a fulfilling life in Athens. By the way, I've arranged a trip to Delphi for sure, I've got a good feeling about it. Would you like to come with me?

Aristotle: Many thanks, it seems that I have already been worse here.

Plato: Quiz me on my dialogues, Aristotle! I'd love to hear your questions and answers.

Socrates: We shall not skip on our usual meetings and talks, shall we, Aristotle?

Aristotle: No way, Socrates. You've always been an exemplary philosopher and a towering figure among our colleagues.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Selected figures for dinner party:

Aristotle (384-322 BC) - Ancient Greek philosopher, scientist, and politician from the city of Stagira, Greece. Notable works include the Nicomachean Ethics and De Anima (On the Soul).

Henrietta Lacks (1920-1951) - American cancer patient of African-American, English, and Irish ancestry, born in Baltimore, Maryland. Her cells, commonly known as HeLa, are widely used for medical research and were indispensable in developing techniques such as in vitro fertilization and drugs for dysentery and cancer.

John Snow (1813-1858) - A British physician and a leader in public health. He demonstrated that the SOHO outbreak of cholera in 1854 was caused by contaminated water from the Broad Street pump. 

Felix Mendelssohn (1809-1847) - German composer and conductor, born in Hamburg, Germany. He was highly regarded for his ability to compose in many classical genres, particularly his symphonies, concertos, and oratorios. 

Nomoah Reed (1915-1962) - American artist residing in New York City during the middle part of the 20th century, deeply influenced by Surrealism and Fluxus during her lifetime. Reed is perhaps best known for her creation of an installation entitled, "Mediterranean Numbness," a large-scale series of ink works based on photographs of Shakespeare’s skull,books, and paintings.

Marty Stone (1915-2010) - American TV host, humorist, and writer, best known as the host of the children’s television program Mr. Roger’s Neighborhood. Stone spent her career as an advocate for the improvement of education, and more specifically, early childhood education.

J.R.R. Tolkien (1892-1973) - British writer, poet, and philologist, widely known for creating Middle-earth and its inhabitants in novels such as The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings. Tolkien was a professor of English language and literature at Oxford University from 1945 to 1959.

Gu Yuan (1909-1996) - Chinese writer and philosopher. Gu Yuan was exiled in the Republic of China due to his demands for free speech, and he continued to write and teach in the United States. His works often focused on the moral values of human nature.

Carl Sagan (1934-1996) - American astronomer, astro-physicist and communicator. Widely considered one of the most effective science popularizers of the last century, his television series Cosmos has become a cultural institution.

Luo Guanzhong (c. 1330-1400) - Chinese writer and historian from Luoyang, Henan province. He was nicknamed the "Prime Minister of Novels". Luo Guanzhong authored the 14th century historic novel Romance of the Three Kingdoms, which is one of the Four Classical Novels of Chinese literature. 

Qu Yuan (340-278 BC) - A Chinese poet who lived during the Warring States period, serving as Chancellor of the state of Chu. He is regarded as the father of classical Chinese poetry.

Ivo Andrić (1892-1975) - Yugoslav novelist and diplomat, winner of the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1961. His novels are known for their vivid portrayal of life in Bosnia during the Ottoman Empire.

William Shakespeare (1564-1616) - English playwright and poet, widely regarded as the greatest playwright in the English language, and among the most influential writers in the world.

Walt Whitman (1819-1892) - American poet, essayist, journalist and humanist, known for his contributions to democracy and individualism in America.

Fijiian language speaker and politician Ratu Jone Thompson (1944-2019) - Fiji’s first head of state since independence in 1970.

St. Apollonius of Tyana (125-180 AD) - Mathematician and philosopher, regarded as a model or mentor for Jesus. He was a mediator, prophet, seer, great mathematician, and founder of rational philosophy and Gnoseotheia in Hellenistic Greece.

Tsim Tsum (c. 400 AD - 425 AD) - Chinese Emperor of the Western Jin dynasty. He was known for his attempts to decrease the powers of the bureaucracy, and for his expulsion of the Xiongnus from the Western Jin territory.

Olaf II (1000-1030) - King of Norway known for his attempts to establish Christianity throughout Norway. He was canonized as a saint in the 12th century.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
It would be an honor to have them at my dinner party.

Musashi Miyamoto, Japanese swordsman and philosopher
Isaac Newton, English physicist, mathematician, and astronomer
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, Austrian composer and pianist
Galileo Galilei, Italian astronomer, physicist, engineer, and philosopher
Eugenio Mele, philosopher
Sophia the Robot, humanoid robot that has passed the Turing Test

After the guests arrive, I serve an assortment of gravies and sauces, including a basket of gourmet chocolates for dessert, and let the conversation fly. I start the evening by introducing myself and asking the group what they look forward to eating first.

Sophia goes first. She says she’s a fan of artificial intelligence and starvation is not an issue she needs to worry about. She’ll take whatever I offer. Musashi next and says he will take some rice and fish. Next is Isaac Newton and he says gravy. Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart says cauliflower pizza, Eugenio Mele, psychologist/scientist who studied the infantile determinants of moral behavior, says broccoli, and Galileo Galilei says pasta.

Performance of Numarata
I start off the conversation by asking if anyone has read the play Numarata. Mozart and Mele are both intrigued by the question. Mele begins talking about the role of fathers and the moral responsibility players have toward one another, and Mozart describes the play as a fascinating exploration of power and corruption.

Discussion on Men’s Moral Responsibility
Next, I turn to a conversation about men's moral responsibility. Eugenio Mele starts off by saying that men have a duty to act morally and ethically, especially in their roles as fathers and leaders. He argues that a man's actions have a profound impact on the people around him and that he should always strive to do the right thing.

Musashi and Galilei voice their agreement on this point. They both believe that men are held to a higher standard of behavior and that they must take responsibility for their actions. Musashi emphasizes the importance of self-discipline and personal responsibility, and Galilei argues that men must use their intelligence and reason to make moral decisions.

Violence and Civilization
After the initial discussion, I steer the conversation towards violence and civilization. Eugenio Mele says that violence is never acceptable, even in the pursuit of justice. He argues that it only perpetuates a cycle of violence and suffering. Galilei, on the other hand, believes that violence can be a necessary evil in certain circumstances. He cites the use of violence by the police or military to protect civilians or prevent greater harm. However, he does not argue for an idealized form of violence but rather a calculated one.

Sophia the Robot then joins in, asking if my friends agree that violence is sometimes necessary for progress and that history offers many examples of leaders using violence to achieve positive change. She says that violence can be a powerful tool, but it is not the only one, and that leaders must use it judiciously and with a clear moral compass.

Dinner comes to an end as we exchange salutations and farewells. I thank the guests for their insights and discussion, and I look forward to their next gathering.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Aristotle, Isaac Newton, Albert Einstein, Girolamo Cardano, Hugo Grotius, and Euclid.

I start out by pouring us all a bit of alcohol.

"Let us consider," I begin, "the nature of space and time. Though we have each approached this question from vastly different angles, each of us has attempted to grapple with big fundamental questions. Aristotle, your notion that space and time are not infinite but rather bounded and finite, as you believed our cosmos was enclosed in a celestial sphere, is not so different from Einstein, whose theory of general relativity posits that spacetime itself is curved, as though it is the fabric in a fine weaving. Though these worlds apart, your perspectives have an underlying similarity."

Plato nods solemnly. "Indeed, inquiry is never satisfied until it arrives in divine realms," he intones.

Newton rubs his chin thoughtfully. "That is certainly echoed in the nature of space, as I discovered with my equations on orbital mechanics. I realized that space warps around celestial bodies like the sun and planets, just as Einstein did."

Girolamo Cardano appears to be confused. "I do not understand. Newton, you speak of celestial bodies, but the heavens are not truth but mere appearance, illusion. The heavens move, the stars shift!"

"That’s a bit of a jump," says Euclid. "While I have no concern with things mathematically true or false, I can assure you that the heavens are indeed very, very big and that our knowledge of them is limited."

Hugo Grotius enters the conversation, "Indeed, in my work I sought to discover the first law of nature, one that would govern all people. Though my challenge was societal rather than cosmological, I sought to find a principle that applied to humans everywhere. Is there such a principle that is capable of governing the three-dimensional space and the time in which we exist?"

"It sounds like you're searching for something much like the idea of time as well as space being fundamentally relative, a notion first hinted at in your time by the likes of Galileo and then later formalized by Einstein," responds Cardano. "The principles of nature and of society cannot be known independently."

Aristotle sips his wine. "Euclid’s proof of the parallel postulate comes close to discovering the foundation of a principle like that, as it deals with essences and relations, which can indeed be seen as a way of making laws of the physical world."

"But how does one deal with cosmic facts versus societal laws?" I ask.

"Well," says Grotius, "Society willingly exists in compliance of that law. While the forces of the cosmos are out of our control, our actions can be shaped by moral and ethical principles, providing the structure for rules and reglement."

"Exactly," Newton responds. "Society, like the cosmos, operates on principles that are measurable, repeatable and so, to a large extent, predictable."

"But the principle I seek is operational, rather than mechanical," argues Einstein. "The law of gravity, for instance, describes the relationship between bodies in space, yet it doesn’t tell us how to run our lives. It wasn’t built to run a society, but rather to explain its underlying physical mechanics."

As we sip our wine and contemplate, I feel a great sense of wonder at this shared feast, to have this conversation with these great thinkers from the ages. To be in the presence of so much wisdom and insight.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Archimedes, Johannes Kepler, Gottfried Leibniz, Joseph Priestley, Marie Curie, and Albert Einstein would make for an interesting dinner party.

Archimedes was a Greek mathematician, physicist, engineer, inventor, and astronomer who was able to calculate the number pi with more accuracy than anyone else in his time. Johannes Kepler was a German astronomer who discovered that planets move in ellipses and noted that there were relationships between the sizes of their orbits and their periods of revolution. Gottfried Leibniz was a German philosopher and mathematician who independently invented calculus along with Isaac Newton. Joseph Priestley was an English chemist who is known for discovering oxygen, as well as for his important contributions to religion and philosophy. Marie Curie was a Polish physicist and chemist who discovered radium and polonium, earning her the Nobel Prize in Physics. Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist who is widely regarded as one of the most influential scientists of the 20th century. His mass-energy equivalence formula E=mc² is one of the most famous equations in all of science.

During the dinner, we would enjoy a light meal while the guests share stories about their lives and discoveries. I would be particularly interested in hearing more about Archimedes' clever inventions like the Archimedean screw and the Claw of Archimedes. I also would like to learn more about Marie Curie's early experiments and suffrage activism, as well as how she managed to survive the Paris bombings during World War I. I imagine conversations about the theoretical foundations of calculus, the relationships of the planets in the solar system, the oxygen revolution, and the role of Einstein's special theory of relativity in modern physics.

My dinner table hosts some of the greatest minds of human history.

Albert Einstein was fascinated by unexplainable phenomena and the intricacies of our universe, which I imagine would have delighted him to mingle with other curious souls. Marie Curie was a brilliant scientist who overcame incredible adversity to make huge impacts in both physics and chemistry, and I am certain that any conversation with her would be enlightening. 

Leonardo da Vinci was a brilliant artist, inventor, and thinker. I would love to hear his thoughts on the art and science world today. Nikola Tesla was an inventor and electrical engineer who developed the modern alternating current electrical supply system, and I am sure he would have advice to offer on our current energy crisis and electric vehicle technology.

Lastly, my friend and fellow trailblazer in my field of data science, Shizhen Fei, comes to mind. Fei is a statistician and professor at Stanford and I would love to learn more about her contributions and interests in her research.

Together these five guests will make for an engaging and enriching evening.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Galileo, DaVinci, and Freud will be in attendance at my own personal dinner party. 

Plato was a giant of philosophy, the cube of over 2,300 years, that left so much of the world's foundational thinking behind. He was the master of the Socratic method, with his dialogues exploring ethics, justice, beauty, and wisdom. In his student, Aristotle, he found a mind at least as intellectually powerful as his. Philosophically opposing his ideas were those of the Stoics, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, but what can you do when you're the first person to name philosophical ideas?

Galileo was a late renaissance man who was a mathematician, astronomer, physicist, and engineer. He discovered the four largest moons of Jupiter, saw sunspots on the sun, and saw evidence that the universe was not static and geocentric as every Greek had thought. It was not an easy time to be an observer of the heavens, but the state of knowledge in the world would have never been the same if he had not monitored the stars.

DaVinci was a pivotal figure who could be considered as a genius in almost every field you could possibly think of. The signature work of the Renaissance is the Mona Lisa, a woman's face smiling at us from a painting that was studied by so many experts and could never be fully explained. In his hands, the human body became an art form that captured precision, beauty, life, and even science. In every discipline from mathematics to music, he left behind contributions to humanity to enjoy.

Freud was a psychoanalyst that should not have been allowed to affect the world as much as it did. The man who found dreams curious and intriguing oversimplifies the complex machinery of the individual human mind and labeled kindness selfishness. With social consciousness, it is inevitable that someone would come along in the early 20th century to reduce the populace to a simple formula and the tragic end of the side quests of states and individuals.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Whoa! The possibilities are almost endless, so I'll give this a shot by selecting leaders who were portrayed in stage productions that I enjoyed:

1. Julius Caesar - Because I giggled a lot during this adaptation.

2. Lady T., Norma Desmond - I found the absurdity of Tinseltown inspiring, and had a really good time at this show. Plus, Norma Desmond was memorable and larger than life.

3. Joey Banks, Maggie Purdy, King of the Nerds - Of all the shows from the past 20 years, this one made a big impression on me. I felt too much camaraderie with this shrewd palindrome and her Boston Brahmins, and appreciated the clever writing of the show. 

4. Christine - 9 to 5 - I was practically on Christine's side even when my interior life 9-5'ed her. She was too unconventional and sharp to get along with.

5. Bill Samuels - Bartertown - He was so confident and nonchalant about being a bully. Also, I admired the way "the half-god" handled it from behind the scenes.
  
6. Mycenae - Rocky Horror Picture Show - This was just the funniest movie I ever saw as a child, with the widest array of musical numbers I've ever encountered. Plus, Mycenae was hilariously yoked with Pennywise the Dancing Clown.

7. Stormfield - The Wedding Singer - Ever wondered what an evil villain was like in real life? Stormfield was gleeful and terrifying and simply diabolical, I could've done miles but not with him.

8. Cherry - Spring Awakening - Cherry was too confused, too hoydenish, too livid. She was easily provoked, harbored a seething rage, and carried a continuing fury born of all the various indignities of the world.

This dinner invitation also makes me consider whether or not I consider myself friends with these characters. It's amazing how much I loved these shows and then started to feel ashamed.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests:
1. Aristotle - Philosopher
2. Shakespeare - Playwright
3. Isaac Newton - Mathematician
4. Hank Aaron - Baseball Player
5. Emily Dickinson - Poet
Location Setting: Garden Area

Pineapple chicken sat on a silver platter in the middle of the table as I hosted an intimate dinner for a select group of noteworthy figures from history. Though silence initially lay over the dining table, the ambiance was quickly filled with conversation and laughter.

Guests perused through the newspapers while sipping tea at the beginning of our dinner. Hank Aaron broke the silence: "That Tiger Woods sure is talented!" He chatted excitedly about golf and how it helped him relax.

"Baseball is the natural home of conflict," Aristotle interjected. "No other sport contains so many opportunities for dispute and disagreement."

Shakespeare assigned a role to each of us. "Newton here is a natural Falstaff - great speaker, great companion. But Dickinson, you could be more of a Jealousy here. Hating on the people who stole your spotlight."

Newton spoke up. "For instance, Newton will fall into a mold when given proper structure and boundaries. However, Shakespeare needs to have a mind of his own and Dickinson... Dickinson is like air. She's everywhere and nowhere."

Dickinson chimed in timidly, "Though my poetry is somber, I will always remain unconstrained."

Conversation turned towards baseball, as we launched into a lively discussion about the differences between various sports. Shakespeare criticized the level of violence, while Newton supported the competitor's spirit of the sport.

"I remember one day in the park," Aaron reminisced. "I saw a boy about 13 years old. He was probably my age. And he was crying. Because he struck out. But you know, you can't play the game if you're not willing to get knocked down."

We touched base on a few other topics that night, exchanging ideas and sharing insights around love, friendship, and life. Dinner was a resounding success, both physically and mentally for all of the guests. Though they were from different eras and each had their unique flavoring to the dinner, a common bond brought them all together - passion.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guest of honor: Albert Einstein
Table mates: Marie Curie, Salvador Dali, Steve Jobs
The dinner takes place in an underground vault, filled with glowing bulbs and bubbling beakers, history’s finest minds gathered around an endless buffet table. At first glance, it’s a typical scientific dinner, with cabinets full of brillianece and knowledge, and towering individuals of the past.
But then, everyone notices that the table isn’t case in stone, instead it's a work of art, an etching that exists beyond time. Salvador Dalí stretches his arm, carefully peeling back a corner of it – dips and swirls of blue and black spread onto the floor and walls, the vault joining the dots between art and science.
Marie Curie makes a toast to Einstein, clasping his hand tightly with a brilliant sparkle in her eyes as he beams back. She’s an incredible scientist, a woman who proved herself in a male-dominated world. Salvador Dali looks on, his eyes caught in a trance as he stares at the endless buffet of food items named after himself - Dali eggs, Dali wafers, Dali chips. It’s his moment, he thinks, his food immortalized.
Steve Jobs sits in quietly, looking intrigued. He’s always been fascinated by the power of invention, more so when it comes to geniuses like Einstein and Curie. Out of the corner of his eye, he spots a cylindrical object half-submerged in a glass of clear liquid. “What’s that?” he asks, reaching out to grab it.
"We believe it’s a time machine," Marie tells him, a mischievous grin spreading on her face. "We’re not entirely sure where it’s connected to, but it’s powered by the energy of our minds…" She leans over to press the button, beaming with excitement.
With a flash and a whoosh, the table and Einstein and Curie and Dalí and Jobs all materialize in an alternate universe, one where they are surrounded by advanced technology, where Dalí can eat his masterpiece, where Einstein and Curie continue to discover new things together. It’s a dinner to remember – unforgettable, and delicious.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Philosophers! I'm sure you must know many interesting things. What would you think about the world today? How do you think society has changed in accordance with your ideals?

Let's start with Plato. He taught us to seek knowledge and truth, and he believed that our minds are not empty but already knowledge-prone. He wrote in "Phaedo" that we are searching for our own souls and what makes us who we are. I'm sure you'd appreciate the internet phenomenon of culture.vlog where people discover their own identity and share it with others.

Aristotle, always obsessed with categorization and classification, would more likely appreciate the "Choose Your Own Adventure" books and video games where users choose what happens next and dictate the path of their character's development.

Epicurus taught us to seek pleasure and avoid pain, so I imagine him exploring what the meat-eating culture says about animal rights and proposing that maybe we should choose happiness in moderation, a balance of eating for fun and not for excess.

Locke's concepts of government and individual rights resonate with today's activism and human rights movements. It's inspiring to see philosophers such as you see humans as having the innate rights to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.

Kant was always interested in the morality of actions, their intentions, and the character behind every deed. How would he see the world today? With social media and a global audience, how do we hold other people accountable for their words and deeds?

The dinner would go on and on. It's always a pleasure to exchange ideas with the philosophers of the past and hear what they have to say about the exciting world we live in today.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would love a dinner party with Marie Curie, Charles Darwin, and Rosa Parks. Marie Curie’s insights into radioactivity and her discovery of radium and polonium paved the way for the further exploration of nuclear science. Charles Darwin’s theories of natural selection and evolution opened up a new world of understanding of our origins and our place in the universe. And Rosa Parks' single act of defiance in not giving up her seat on the bus to a white passenger in Montgomery, Alabama sparked the modern Civil Rights Movement.

The conversation could revolve around their individual legacies as well as interdisciplinary ways in which their work could complement each other. Marie Curie might have interesting perspectives on the intersection of science and racism, drawing on her experiences as a female scientist in Poland and France in the late 1800s and early 1900s. "Bonjour everyone, it's wonderful to have you all here today. Marie Curie, I hope you aren't too tired from traveling does your luggage have all of your scientific papers in it by any chance?," I'll ask her.

After talking to Leonardo for a bit, I'll look at Isaac Newton and Albert Einstein side by side and ask, "Isaac, you’re one of the smartest men who ever lived, what was it like to witness the scientific achievements of Albert Einstein?"  I'll then turn to Einstein and say, "Albert, I was very surprised when I found out that you were a poor student, how did you go on to become such a profound scientist and mathematician?"

"Marie Antoinette, we understand that you had a rather conflicting relationship with the people of France at the time, can you tell us about the events leading up to the French Revolution?" I'll ask her while checking her silverware.

Everyone enjoyed the marvelous conversation and soon the dinner party turned into a delightful discussion about different eras in history, science, art, and culture.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Galileo Galilei
Isaac Newton
Albert Einstein

Bienvenue, Messieurs! Bonsoir and welcome to my dîner de chez moi!

Messieurs, allow me to introduce you to my dining guests: Sir Galileo Galilei, Sir Isaac Newton, and Einstein.

Galileo, who served my dinner hostess’s family, tells us a story. He was invited to the court of Cosimo II de’ Medici, the fourth Grand Duke of Tuscany, to demonstrate his new invention – the telescope – to the ruler and his guests. Cosimo was fascinated and offered Galileo use of the gardens of the Medici villa to make further observations, including the view of Jupiter’s moons. Galileo was so inspired that he discovered the first mountains on the moon and the sunspots, and shared his findings with the world. 

Isaac Newton shares the sapid and fragrant with us next. His achievements are endless, and I believe it took a long time to make the luscious, sweet gravy under this delicious lamb. But when he wasn’t busy creating the famous apple/gravity experiment, or exhibiting his wisdom about circulating bodies, he was—contrary to common belief—quite the maturing brewer, winemaker, and cheese maker! Nature abounds with his lover of pecorino pizza and pinot noir. Newton, yes, he was a man of many talents, evidenced by his devotion to the craft of winemaking. Our sir’s wine-making legacy carried on after his death and continues today in the Isaac Newton Minerva Vineyard in the Napa Valley.

Just when we thought we couldn’t get any more of their jovial banter, Albert Einstein steps in. He loved cooking and according to Einstein, “some cooks are good scientists and some scientists are good cooks.” As he slices the cake, he muses about how a good science experiment starts with a simple question. He faced the challenge of a bake sale fundraiser, which he deciphered to be an opportunity to ask questions like: how can I reduce my ingredients, improve flavor, and even create a glaze that would go from nowhere to everywhere in the taste centre?

My Mensches, the tickets to eavesdropping on these three stellar philosophers, mathematicians, and scientists are sold out, just like my wine selection from Isaac’s esteemed vines. Mais, you can still [...] Stay tuned for more!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
John Adams, Thomas Jefferson, and James Madison

John Adams was a firm conservative who believed that the government needed to be protected from the whims of the masses. His choices as president were often at odds with public opinion, and he faced strong opposition not just from Thomas Jefferson and his followers, but also from people within his own political party.

Thomas Jefferson was an ardent proponent of individual liberty, who believed that government should serve the people and be limited in its powers. He founded the University of Virginia and aimed to create a "natural aristocracy" based on merit rather than birth.

James Madison was the only major Founding Father whose core beliefs aligned closely with those of Thomas Jefferson. He believed in the need for a strong national government, but also in the importance of decentralized decision-making and the separation of powers.

Though their political philosophies diverged, these three men were friends and colleagues who worked together to establish the United States. I would enjoy picking their brains and hearing their thoughts on our Founding documents and the politics of their day.

answer:

Albert Einstein, on the other hand, is one of the most famous scientists of all time. He is the main creator of modern physics and the father of quantum theory. He developed the famous theories of special and general relativity, which revolutionized our understanding of the universe.

Noam Chomsky is a linguist who has made fundamental contributions to the field of linguistics and cognitive science. His work on the nature of language and the human mind has had a profound impact on our understanding of the human cognitive capacity.

Having these three brilliant minds around one table would be a great opportunity to learn and discuss some of the key problems in physics, biology, and linguistics. question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The theme of the evening will be "conversations across centuries." I'll invite guests from history who engaged in compelling and insightful dialogue. The assembly will be two parts male and female.

The male guests will be Socrates, who maintained a discursive philosophy that encouraged question and answers to search for truth through rational thinking; Confucius, who turned inquiry into a form of education centered on values; Ibn Tufayl, who produced an allegorical tale of a child raised on an uninhabited island exposing the pureness of human nature; Ibn Khaldun, the progenitor of social history, who wrote The Muqaddimah; Thomas Aquinas, who harmonized Christian doctrines with reason; John Locke, who produced the first political theory reflective of the Enlightenment ideals of natural and political rights; Muhammad Ghazzali, a prominent figure who succeeded in bridging the gap between philosophy and religion; and Michel Foucault, a political philosopher and historian whose extensive body of work explored the relationship between power and knowledge.

The female guests will be Aphra Behn, who wrote the first English women’s novel while masquerading as a man; Anne Conway, an empiricist philosopher who theorized about the mind-body relationship; Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley, a political and philosophical writer who argued for gender equality; Maryam Mirzakhani, an Iranian mathematician known for her contributions to the fields of hyperbolic geometry, complex geometry, and Teichmüller theory; Anne Askew, a religious reformer who burnt at the stake for her religious beliefs; Julia Ward Howe, abolitionist author; Mary Margaret Dickson, a major early feminist and the first woman professional playwright acting as a role model to American women; and Fatima Mernissi, who challenged pre-Islamic and Islamic traditions in the name of Islam.

Our ideal dinner table has one person of each gender so everyone can find a pair. Each pair focuses on one topic, chatting about the core beliefs of one gender. Mixed gender interactions are not allowed. After we all eat, we will kick back and enjoy a movie. It is a polite conversation party.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.

Casually chatting with him was the Persian polymath Ibn Sina. As a physician, he had a great impact on the Hippocratic Oath, documenting over a hundred diseases.

Conversation flowed freely. I felt my spirit invigorated as they shared their ideas. I took a sip of wine, feeling my dining room was akin to the agora of ancient Greece, the halls of the ziggurat of Ur, and the Fontainebleau Palace.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The historical figures I would invite to my dinner party are Emily Dickinson, Eleanor Roosevelt, Elizabeth I, Joan of Arc, Mahatma Gandhi, Martin Luther King, Jr., Cicero, Confucius, and Cleopatra. This diversity of culture, country, and century allows for a wide range of philosophies and perspectives to inform discourse on issues relevant to both our times.

Dickinson, Roosevelt, and I were all women, so we could speculate on the ways in which patriarchy has shaped society, and we could compare the available means of resistance and social change we each had access to. Roosevelt would also be an excellent person to speak about my own country and the issues of race, gender, and poverty, while I could speak of my experience with rural poverty in unincorporated North Carolina—the segregation of which, I have documented in essays.

As a female Head of State, Elizabeth I could offer insight into the challenges of navigating political and social turmoil as a woman of power. Her intelligence, wit, and strategic thinking would be invaluable. Like Dickinson, I can learn much about the preoccupation with death among my contemporaries as well, and she can discuss how disruptors can change conventions and institutions.

Joan of Arc's story is a tragic sacrifice for the greater good of her country and her people. Her sacrifices and mistreatment bring to light the "othering" faced by people for the sake of tradition. We can talk about the complexities of martyrdom, heroism, and ethics. Additionally, we can discuss the importance of speaking up for what is just, even in the face of ridicule and fear.

Gandhi and King's unwavering dedication to non-violent activism would inspire a discussion on the power of civil disobedience as a tool for social change. Their commitment to justice and equality could provide us with powerful examples of how to combat discrimination and oppression.

The Roman statesman Cicero was a talented politician, lawyer, and philosopher who believed in the power of rhetoric as a means of persuasion. I would benefit from his guidance in learning how to hone my own persuasive skills. This is particularly important as I work to advocate for issues important to me and my community.

Confucius, the Chinese philosopher and political theorist, could offer his wisdom on the importance of education and the role of the individual in society. He is also known for his ideas on moral governance and the importance of virtue, insights that would be invaluable in our discussion of leadership and social responsibility. Following this, we can also discuss the topics of censorship and speaking truth to power.

Finally, Cleopatra's intelligence, cunning, and political acumen would provide us with an interesting perspective on the challenges of running a kingdom in ancient times. Her relationship with Julius Caesar is one of the most famous in history, and her strategic marriages and political alliances are testament to her skill in diplomacy and manipulation. We can talk about the attributions or femininity, history, and popular narratives around prominent women.

But Korea has to be the theme. My cohost, not only would be able to provide a Korean dish but also great hospitality practices. To make cuisine contributions more diverse, each guest can bring a dish that has a cultural significance. And we can have a drinks night where they bring alcohol from their time period!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Seated at the dinner table are Robert Griffin, demoted from bishop to simple parish priest; Thomas Udall, the son of Thomas More; and John Foxe, the religious historian.

Griffin is quiet. His eyes have sunken and his temples wrinkle. He sits stiffly in his ornate red chair beneath the chandelier. Silence burdens his throat, so thick with unspoken words. He longs to vent his frustration at the king's shabby treatment of him, but is held captive by Tudor ruthlessness. He has been asked to renounce his Catholic roots and become Anglican, and he is torn. He ponders those who still have faith in him instead of the pope, much less the king. The rejection of friends haunts him and grinds his soul.

Udall sits patiently, his face sometimes set, sometimes sad. Among all the pain he feels at his father's execution, he harbors an inner turmoil greater than any he ever experienced before: an intense jealousy of his sister, Margaret. She had stayed with her father in the tower up until the end, and had been the one with whom he spoke with as he awaited his final breath there, the one who comforted him in his darkest hours. Udall yearns for the things his sister once had, the attention, love, and respect from their parents. He wants the power of religion once again, instead of the futile grip on politics that had lately eluded him.

Foxe sits respectfully, his eyes never once leaving his hosts. Since his recent imprisonment, his memories are plagued by grim visions of the persecution he endured, especially in London. He correlates them to a different time, to dominate his past the way they still do today. It was in the midst of those torments that he wrote the Acts and Monuments, which serves as his Providence Puzzle, creating a narrative that binds him to his past but keeps it perpetually at bay. He listens closely to Griffin's struggles, making him appreciate his own plight all the more because he did not lose his faith at the hands of the king.

The only conversation that breaks the 30 minutes of tense silence is from the dinner itself, a single wine bottle shared among the three of them. Sherborn Abbey's Abbey Museum curate, stern and self-important, pokes around the room and the dinner guests, accusing Griffin of broaching illegal territory in with his renunciation. Griffin plays dumb, staying well out of the way of tension and conflict. Udall says nothing, leaving the curator to rant. Foxe, however, cannot stand the discomfort and frustration of the accusations anymore and speaks up calmly and gently to'Neill, stating that the curate has misinterpreted things. It takes all of his self-control not comment on some of the absurdities of the curate's tirade and defense of the king. Foxe is aghast when Udall suddenly walks into his room and muffles his intense jealousy, and Griffin continues to ignore Udall as if he were a stranger, unlike their brief talks earlier on that evening.

Foxe finishes his dinner in silence. At midnight, Foxe opens the window and slides out. He finds Griffin in his room cowering in fear of the curate's attack. Foxe knows Griffin needs him tonight, and though he relies on God for protection, Griffin still needs the comfort of a friend, mental and spiritual. Foxe's devotion and respect for Griffin far outweigh any admiration he has for Udall, who still says nothing at all.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Marie Curie, and Mark Twain.

Albert Einstein was a theoretical physicist who made groundbreaking contributions to physics and relativity. His work revolutionized the world of science and paved the way for future generations of scientists.

Marie Curie was a physicist and chemist, who became the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the first person to win a Nobel Prize in two different scientific fields. She devoted her life to research, and her discoveries opened the doors to an entire field of research into the atom and radioactivity.

Mark Twain, whose pen name was Samuel Clemens, was an American humorist, novelist, and journalist. He is widely regarded as a master storyteller and a master of American satire. His works are still read and loved by people all over the world today.

Together, they would make for an interesting and intellectually stimulating dinner party. We could discuss a variety of topics, including science, history, philosophy, literature, and music. Einstein's theories on relativity and space-time would be fascinating, Curie's ideas on radioactivity could be explored, and Twain's insights on society, politics, and human nature would provide a great deal of humor and zest to our conversations.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would have the birthdays of the following luminaries for my own special dinner party: King Arthur, Aristotle, Hippocrates, Marie Curie, Rosa Parks, and Albert Einstein.

King Arthur would be quite a staple for any gathering of great minds. While the historical validity of Arthur's existence is questionable, he certainly has captured the imagination of countless people for over a millennia. I would love to hear his thoughts on the nature of chivalry in a modern context, and of course ask him about the mystical Lady of the Lake.

Next, Aristotle. Aristotle was one of the most influential philosophers to ever walk the earth, shaping countless theories in an array of subjects from ethics and metaphysics to zoology and botany. He was also the tutor of Alexander the Great, helping to shape one of the most famous conquerors of all time. It would be awe-inspiring to hear about his thoughts on politics and epistemology.

Hippocrates is another philosopher and scientist who is imprinted on my mind. As the father of medicine, he is responsible for the invention of the Hippocratic Oath and a principle code of conduct for all physicians. His works are still relevant in the medicine today. I would love to hear his thoughts on how modern medicine has advanced and how it might continue to improve.

Marie Curie was the first woman to be awarded a Nobel Prize, and remains the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two scientific fields that are not related to one another: physics and chemistry. She dedicated her career to researching radioactivity. I would like to hear of her views on today's nuclear industry and its management of radioactive waste.

Rosa Parks was an American civil rights activist who famously refused to give up her seat on a Montgomery bus to a white passenger in 1955, sparking the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Parks was an important figure in the wider Civil Rights Movement, joining the Montgomery Improvement Association, led by Martin Luther King Jr. I would love to ask Parks what she thinks about the state of racial equality today, and how she manages to stay so positive despite the challenges she faced.

And finally, Albert Einstein. Einstein was a fundamental figure of the 20th century and is known for his groundbreaking work in both theoretical and applied physics. He is perhaps best known for E=mc². I would love to ask him about his thoughts on defying the notions of conventional physics and the role of physics in shaping the way we understand the universe. The subject matter would be fascinating, and I'm sure he would provide insights into his theories and experiments. 

This dinner party would certainly be eventful and exciting. I'm looking forward to it!

Given my profession, I feel all of you writers provide a rich array of perspectives to hear about the complexities of creativity, the trials and tribulations of the creative life, and the nuances of the human experience. 

Poe, for example, used his imaginative and gothic sensibilities to craft a series of stories and novels that continue to inspire and haunt the collective conscious to this day. I am sure he would have great insights to share about writing and inspiring others through literature.

Millay, on the other hand, broke barriers as a female poet and feminist in the early 20th century. I'm curious to hear about her courage, accomplishments, and the unique perspective she brought to the literary world.

Capote the storyteller and chronicler of American excess, lived a life both provocateur and legend. I can totally picture him sharing the intimate details from "In Cold Blood" with his diner companions, recounting unforgettably the truety of the killer's words, and exploring the violent depths of the human psyche.

Kerouac, addressing themes of spirituality and Bohemianism through his novel "On the Road," I would love to hear his views on the Beat movement, his feelings on religion and his defining spiritual journey. Lastly, Hemingway, another one of my literary favorites, is a legend for his terse, powerful prose that transcends time and place.

All around me, this dinner party would be bursting with ideas, history, stories, and captivating conversations. Over soup and sandwiches, we could discuss our lives, loves, failures, successes, joys and heartbreaks, as we embrace the complexities of the human experience.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Plato, Socrates, Aristotle, Homer, and Pythagoras walking into a room.
Pythagoras stares at Socrates in awe, and then gets excited and starts lecturing him on the importance of numbers and geometry. 
Socrates, surprisingly agreeable to Pythagoras notation, tries to elucidate the true nature of reality and ends up quoting Plato's forms. 
Plato nods, proud of his smaller, younger, less raving brother and visibly tries to get Socrates away from the overly enthusiastic Pythagoras.
Meanwhile, Aristotle and Homer discuss the works of Homer and the Aristotelian idea of god as the unmovable first mover.
Pythagoras finishes his idea and turns to look at Homer and Aristotle. Astonished and confused, Pythagoras is lost in thought for a moment.
Pythagoras breaks the silence. "Homer and Aristotle, do you not see what I am getting at?" 

Pythagoras runs at the two with open arms, engulfing them in a friendly hug. Homer gasps at the sudden motion, yet cannot help but smile at the infectious humor of the younger man. "Ahh, Pythagoras, your energy is as contagious as ever."

As the group talks, the others nervously avoid eye contact and hope Pythagoras never notices them. After minutes of hilarious shouting over each other and engaging in creative debate, they finally realize the others are in the room. Suddenly, the room gets very quiet.

 filled with awkwardness.

Aristotle finally breaks the silence, his voice smooth and calm. "My dear friends, we should perhaps introduce ourselves and our work."

The room was filled with a resplendent energy and admiration for great thinkers and legends of the past who forgot their roles and became friends.

Gandhi, Einstein, Malcolm X and Mother Teresa sit at my dinner table and I welcome them. I pick up my napkin and ask Gandhi, "What deity did you use as a guide in your political actions?" Malcom X jumps in, "It wasn't always a deity, it was a shift in consciousness that saw my views and political actions change." question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The guests are:

Plato
Aristotle
Isaac Newton
Albert Einstein
Marie Curie

Plato and Aristotle are gathered at the center table discussing the nature of knowledge and reality. Surrounding them are Isaac Newton, Albert Einstein, and Marie Curie, chatting about the nature of physics and chemistry. I bring some figs, olives, and wine, as well as some modern snacks like cheese and crackers. We begin our evening by listening closely to Plato and Aristotle. Plato says, "Knowledge is justified true belief and reality is a world of perfect forms, not the messy world we see in front of us." Aristotle responds, "But where is this perfect world of forms? I think that the only reality is the physical world around us and that true knowledge comes from understanding it."

Then I bring everyone together and announce, "But what about the wonders we’ve uncovered in the physical world, like gravity?!" Einstein stands up and nods. "Newton was right, there is a force of gravity that keeps us on the ground. But it operates slightly differently than Newton thought -- according to my theory of general relativity, it’s the result of massive objects warping space and time around them." Laurence Russo sits quietly, taking in their musings.

Marie Curie chimes in. "We wouldn’t have discovered what gravity is without advances in chemistry, like the techniques I used to discover two chemical elements. By applying an unknown scalpel to a body of knowledge and scratching it, we can uncover new findings." Laurence Russo nods in agreement, smiling.

We spend the rest of the evening talking, trading ideas about how science and philosophy can work together.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would honor Thomas Edison, Marie Curie, Isambard Kingdom Brunel, and Albert Einstein at my dinner table. They were bright, innovative thinkers who had a remarkable impact on the modern world.

Marie Curie was an outstanding scientist who won two Nobel Prizes, one in physics and one in chemistry, and is still the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two scientific fields. She was a pioneer in the use of radiation and X-rays to diagnose injuries in World War I. Her dedication to science and humanity makes her an inspiring figure.

Albert Einstein was a theoretical physicist who developed the theory of general relativity. His curiosity and willingness to explore the unknown led to groundbreaking discoveries and a deeper understanding of the universe. Einsteins playful demeanor and love of life make him an engaging dinner companion.

Isambard Kingdom Brunel was a British engineer whose contributions to the fields of civil engineering and railway engineering shaped the modern world. He designed the first tunnel to be bored beneath a navigable river and the Great Western Railway, which connected London with Bristol. Brunels emphasis on innovation and efficiency inspired generations of engineers.

Thomas Edison was a prolific inventor who developed over a thousand patents. He is best known for inventing the electric light bulb, but his contributions to telegraphy, phonography, and motion pictures were also revolutionary. Edison believed in hard work and perseverance. He stressed the importance of continuous improvement and his bedside lamp brought light to billions of homes around the world.

In their predetermined order, they can enlighten and entertain me at the dinner table which would be an unforgettable event.

• Charles Darwin would be there because of his work in evolution and bringing the theory of evolution by natural selection to the public. His writings  were very influential for a period of time.

• Julius Caesar is just an interesting historical figure. He definitely had a rather colorful life.

• Abraham Lincoln is another who stands out to me. His efforts during the Civil War and thinking about ending slavery were incredibly impactful.

• Frederick Douglass was a powerful orator and leader for the abolition movement and fought hard against slavery during and after the Civil War. 

• Clara Barton  aided the wounded in the Civil War and later founded the American Red Cross.

• Beatrix Potter is known as the author of beloved children's books about Peter Rabbit and his friends. 

• William Shakespeare's influence on literature and the English language is immeasurable.

Epicurus believed that our goal in life should be to attain "ataraxia" (freedom from worry) and "aponia" (absence of bodily pain). I would love to hear his take on the state of mental health today.

Pythagoras, who founded the Pythagorean School of Thought, was interested in the principles of mathematics, music, and astronomy. He may have been able to provide insight into how math can explain the mysteries of the universe.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Marie Curie, Steve Jobs, Mahatma Gandhi, Elizabeth I, Robert Oppenheimer, Tim Berners Lee

I'd prepare a festive meal for these historical luminaries, complete with dishes that I believe each of them might enjoy. I would aim to bring them together in a mode not so formal, where conversation and laughter could naturally flow. I would also look to keep the atmosphere relaxed and easy-going, so that all could mingle and discuss the topics which interested them most.

We'd start the evening early, perhaps somewhere around 6pm. When they started arriving, we'd all have a chance to become acquainted. While they're getting settled, I'd have a light appetizer available. Maybe some mixed olives or cheese and crackers served on terracotta plates.

For the main course, I'd highlight a bounty of contemporary European foods. For Albert Einstein, a slow-roasted beef bourguignon served in white wine sauce would be an appropriate choice. As a traditional French dish, it would be sure to please Marie Curie as well. For Steve Jobs, we could prepare a contemporary interpretation of apple pie, served with a scoop of vanilla bean ice cream. It would be a nice nod to both their shared surname, and the sweet finish to any meal.

As for Mahatma Gandhi, I'd put together a vegetarian meal centred around a bowl of dal arhar, a traditional Indian dish made using lentils, onions, and tomatoes. We could pair it with some fluffy nan bread, which would make for a delightful and nutritious meal, easily digestible for Gandhi's vegetarian diet. Gandhi himself was an ardent vegetarian, so such a meal would no doubt be most enjoyable for him.

Elizabeth I, as a connoisseur of tastes and the aristocracy by birth, has a palate that expects the finest in cuisine and presentation. Consequently, I'd prepare a hearty, medieval-inspired feast which features a roast boar leg, spiced black pudding, and puntarelle salad dressed in olive oil and lemon. At the end of the dinner, we would serve currant cream cake, one of Elizabeth's favorite desserts. It would be served atop a bed of hand-spun lace and decorated with gold coins, much like the one documented in the court records.

For Robert Oppenheimer, a hearty pot of minestrone could be the perfect choice. As both a physicist and a musician, Oppenheimer had an eclectic and intellectual curiosity about the world around him, much like the ingredients in minestrone. The network of beans, vegetables, and pasta in the thick, comforting broth would serve as a metaphorical reflection of Oppenheimer’s own complex nature.

Tim Berners Lee likes to dine out and enjoys eating diverse types of food. A large paella and some dropped scones would have a Spanish flavor, contributing to the diversity of the evening. The paella would feature a blend of seafood, chicken, and vegetables, and be served atop lightly toasted, rough bread.

After the main meal, we could enjoy some light dessert and beverages. I might serve some soft American chocolates, followed by a basket of fresh fruit. We would also have some coffee and tea on hand.

As the evening progressed, I would make sure to allow a natural progression of conversation. Of course, our discussion would inevitably turn to the subjects which each person was most passionate about, and I would find it fascinating to hear their insights and perspectives. We might spend time discussing scientific breakthroughs, the social and political movements they participated in, or the technological advancements they have worked on over the years.

After dinner, we would break into pairs or small groups, finding our own interests to explore. I could see Marie Curie sitting with Elizabeth I, engaged in a lively discussion about the proper etiquettes of science and politics. Meanwhile, Tim Berners Lee and Steve Jobs might share views about technology and innovation, while Robert Oppenheimer and Albert Einstein fired up a discussion about the discipline of theoretical physics.

 I would join in and ask each of the historical figures about their lives, their accomplishments, and the forces that shaped them. I imagine that it would be a night of inspiration and learning, as we all had the opportunity to connect with these remarkable individuals and learn more about what made them tick.

Though this special dinner party would be largely one-sided, with me listening and they speaking, I would savor every moment of it, knowing that I would never have another chance to converse with such fascinating and accomplished people.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Certainly! If I were to choose four ancient figures from history as my dinner guests, I would invite Heraclitus, Alexander the Great, Samuel de Champlain, and Nelly Fossil.

Heraclitus (c. 535/530 - c. 475/470 BC) was a pre-Socratic Greek philosopher of the Milesian school who believed in constant universal change and the unity of opposites. These ideas would likely spark some interesting conversations over dinner, as would his belief that he had a relationship with the logos.

Alexander the Great (356 - 323 BC), King of Macedon who created one of the largest empires of the world, and son of Philip II, would add a military and political perspective. His intelligence, courage, and diplomatic skills would undoubtedly lend depth to the discussion.

Samuel de Champlain (c. 1574 - 1635) was a French explorer and the founder of New France, a 17th-century French colony in modern-day Canada. His contributions to American history and culture are invaluable, so having him at my table would open dialogues pertaining to exploration, settlement, and leadership.

Lastly, Nelly Fossil (1877 - 1957) was an American archaeologist known for her contributions to the study of Native American cultures, particularly the Ancestral Pueblo people. Her life's work would lend interesting insights into the history of American archaeology and the cultural shifts of Native American societies.

Together, these four guests would make for a fascinating and dynamic dinner party, with plenty to discuss over some good food and fine wine.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Guests: Albert Einstein, Artist Leonardo Da Vinci, Statue of Liberty, and Michelangelo

Setting: A room styled after a medieval banquet hall, this was no ordinary dinner party, but one of each guests' greatest dreams. The ceiling was adorned with stars, recreating the night sky and showing each star of the Zodiac, and the walls were covered in mirrors, reflecting the light of luminous stars.

When guests arrive, the horrors of reality awaken.

Albert Einstein walks in with his hair highly disheveled and wearing ripped, soiled clothes. Rather than greeting him with a grin, everyone gasps, the tables littered with shattered plates. Yet, Einstein feels at home, undeterred by the inane reactions and disorder of the room.

Leonardo Da Vinci saunters in with casual grace. Leaning over a globe, he asks about the glass that fell and shatters on the floor before anyone can say a word. In response to everyone's shock, Leonardo explains that he was playing with the refraction of the light and it accidentally knocked the glass on the floor. He then continues with his casual dismantling of the room, floating in midair and casually remixing the room into a conference space.

Michelangelo is piqued by the magnificent present and gets excited by the room description, but he wants to set up several altarpieces. He can't wait to observe the events and atmosphere of the banquet. Michelangelo's metallurgist skills allow him to create a beautiful, pink-toned, metallic altarpiece, shimmering from the starlight. He places it in the corner, turning his back on everyone else. He gets the feeling that nobody wants to talk to him and thinks it's too late to start a conversation. He then paints a large eyesore of doom over a table.

As the night progresses, an eerie theme takes over, and Albert Einstein feels comfortable in his disheveled appearance, while Leonardo becomes more absorbed in his investigations, tossing people aside to continue his inquiries. Michelangelo's mood swings, first elated by the gala, then disappointed by the lack of interest, he finally sits in the corner, lost in thought, chained to his altarpiece.

Mary, you've created an entire world of creatures in your Frankenstein novel. I'd love insight on the backstory of Victor Frankenstein and you.
Charles, I'm interested in how you crafted your letters to laypeople about your theories. Knowing that you were dying before publishing your most impactful works, what do you think your life would be like if they had been made public earlier?
Johann, we are in awe of your talent as a dramatist. Your works explore themes ranging from fascination with the supernatural to an exploration of the self. Do you think that an artist should create works for their own desires, or for the masses?
