Steve Jobs and his wife Laurene
Carl Sagan and his wife Ann


The aperitif was a prosecco and egg roll appetizer - perfect for the brilliant minds who could explore any detail in the world around them, the dishes a combination of Western influence and eastern delicacy.

When the hors d'oeuvres were cleared, Ann entertained everyone with Carl's love for the cosmos, its boundless possibilities, and all it had to offer humanity. As she talked, her eyes sparkled with excitement about the way his research and work had changed the way we see the universe, from its smallest particles to its farthermost anomalies.


Laurene, ever poised and self-possessed, spoke next. She began by saying, "Steve, as life goes, it is always full of ups and downs. But one thing remains constant, and that's the power of creativity and technology to transform the world." Then, Steve went in with an endearing anecdote about how a wounded turtle named Bandit had made him realize the value of humility and the importance of listening to others.


Diego Rivera continued sharing the stories of Frida's eccentricities, her love for life, and her passion for art. He also gave the guests a glimpse into the hard life they both lived and how their relationship survived. Later Elsa shared the stories of Albert's impenetrable intellect and how he used his knowledge to make the world a better place.


As the conversation turned to world-changing technologies like the internet, Distributed Ledger Technology, and the application of blockchain, the two eldest guests, the Wright brothers, were stumped.


Then Steve got up and said, "There was a time when the tallest structures humans could build were perhaps 100 meters. And then the Wright brothers aviation achievement set humans loose into another dimension entirely, freedom of flight. Then the computer revolution came, allowing humans to have access to an almost boundless amount of information. And finally, the open-source internet. By removing barriers and unleashing the ingenuity of people, the progress humanity can make is infinite.


Ann's explanation entranced the guests seated around the table. "So, when I think about our revolutions, it wasn't the internet but blockchain being built on an open-source internet that interests me the most. It's an attempt to bring information back to the people—to decentralize power and move humanity forward. I'm optimistic about blockchain. This technology holds the potential to shift the way we operate our lives and businesses."

Mohandas Gandhi's voice rang out as he spoke about what it means to be human. "If history was taught in the form of stories, it would never be forgotten," he began. "And in America, history is written in blood." Then, from the chair next to him, Kasturbai quietly reminded him that while history shapes us, it's our choosing to move forward wisely that defines us."

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
My dinner party attendees would include Albert Einstein, Picasso, Marilyn Monroe, Charles Darwin, Malala Yousafzai, and Buddha. 

We'd begin with Buddha/Ihso Son, the founder of Buddhism, and his teachings on compassion, wisdom, and impermanence. As the dinner progresses, we'd move on to discuss science with Albert Einstein, artistic innovation with Picasso, literary adaptation with Charles Darwin, and feminism with Malala Yousafzai.

Marilyn Monroe was always a character that I enjoyed talking about, and she would be a delight at the table, if only because she was famously challenging and brilliant. I could have a lengthy conversation with her about Hollywood Trickery, Art and Sexuality. 

Topical and dull would never be the words to describe this dinner party.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Prenatal scanning technology has allowed us to observe a fetus in the womb, but unfortunately, it is not possible for fetuses to attend a dinner party. However, I can certainly arrange a dinner party for you with a set of interesting figures from history! Here are my choices, along with a little bit of background information on each of them:

1. Shakespeare - one of the most renowned playwrights in history. He lived in London during the Elizabethan and Jacobean periods (1564-1616) and wrote 38 plays, 154 sonnets and 2 narrative poems.

2. Marie Curie - a Polish physicist and chemist born in Warsaw in 1867. She won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903 and again in 1911 for her work on radioactivity. Curie and her husband Pierre Curie, along with Henri Becquerel, were awarded the prize for their joint research into the effects of radiation on photographic plates and other materials.

3. Nikola Tesla - a Serbian-American inventor, electrical engineer, mechanical engineer, and futurist. Born in 1856 in Smiljan, Croatia, Tesla is best known for his contributions to the design of the modern alternating current (AC) electricity supply system.

4. Einstein - a German-born theoretical physicist who developed the theory of relativity, one of the two pillars of modern physics. Born in Ulm in 1879, Einstein became one of the most influential scientists of the twentieth century. His formula E=mc² has become one of the most famous equations in the world.

5. Nelson Mandela - the anti-apartheid revolutionary and politician who served as President of South Africa from 1994 to 1999. Having been the country's prisoner of conscience for 27 years, Mandela fought for human rights and equality, winning the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993.

How does that sound? I hope these individuals would have an interesting conversation around the dinner table!

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
The guest list to my dinner party would include Confucius, Mahatma Gandhi, Nelson Mandela, Leonardo da Vinci, Marie Curie, and Albert Einstein.

These figures represent a diverse group of impactful and inspiring individuals whose contributions and accomplishments have shaped our world. 

Confucius was a Chinese philosopher and politician who founded the philosophical school of Confucianism and established the virtues of the scholar-ruler. His teachings on ethics, morals, and the ideal government continue to influence Chinese and Asian society, as well as the world at large.

Mahatma Gandhi was an Indian lawyer, anti-imperialist, philosopher, and political ethicist who was the leader of India's movement for independence from British colonial rule. He is credited with developing the philosophy of nonviolent resistance, which has been embraced by individuals and movements all over the world.

Nelson Mandela was a South African anti-apartheid revolutionary, politician, and philanthropist who served as President of South Africa from 1994 to 1999. He worked tirelessly to end the era of apartheid, and after spending 27 years in prison, he became the country's first black head of state.

Leonardo da Vinci was an Italian polymath whose areas of interest included painting, sculpting, architecture, science, music, mathematics, engineering, literature, anatomy, geology, astronomy, botany, writing, history, and cartography. His ability to connect these disciplines played a key role in his many groundbreaking achievements and innovations.

Marie Curie was a pioneering physicist and chemist who conducted radioactive studies and is credited with the discovery of radium and polonium. A two-time Nobel laureate, she was a champion for women in science and an ardent pacifist who helped establish radiological facilities to aid wounded soldiers throughout World War I.

Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist who is widely regarded as one of the most influential scientists of the 20th century. His theory of relativity revolutionized our understanding of physics, and his famous mass-energy equivalence formula, E=mc², remains one of the most recognizable equations in science.

Getting to converse with these remarkable individuals would undoubtedly be a fascinating and enriching experience.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:

Leonardo: Oh, absolutely! I've never seen a space eatery like this before.

Pythagoras: Oh (excitedly) We can discuss the shapes of black holes, and maybe even the beginning of the universe.
Albert: No, that was a joke. But yes, we can if everyone agrees. (looks at Marie) Marie, why haven't you sat down?
Marie: Oh (distracted) Fortunately, while contemplating transuranic elements, it slipped my mind.
Leonardo: (CONFUSED) Wait, Marie studied what?
Albert: She developed radium and polonium, and discovered nuclear radioactivity and the idea that radioactive elements release energy.
Marie: Yes and we can talk about that at dinner.

Leonardo: I should take that unfinished painting of you, Albert...
Albert: (alarmed) I'm a black hole! What do you want to paint of me?
Leonardo: (laughs) Just thought it'd be a great idea to have some inspiration, eh...
Albert: Inspiration for what?
Leonardo: Oil paintings, unlike you, I'm obsessed.

Marie: (cough) Um, excuse me.
Everybody looks at Marie.
Marie: Uh, hum...
Albert: You're coughing. (drinks water for Marie) Here, take a drink.
Marie: Thanks Alger.

Pythagoras: So, something interesting has occurred to me. If you take a wooden pin and hammer it into a wooden board, it will remain there.
Albert: Yeah...?
Pythagoras: Suppose, however, the pin and the board were both at the centre of the board, the hinges would allow the pin to move about rather than force it into the wood.
Albert: Pythagoras... What are you trying to tell me?
Pythagoras: If we were to somehow engineer a group of black holes around a fixed point, we could create a stable group of them oscillating around their fixed point.
Albert: (impressed) You know what? That's an incredible thought. Stable black holes oscillating around a fixed point. You've opened up a whole new chapter in astrophysics.

Marie: I'm hungry!
Albert: Marie, it's dinner time. Everyone, let's get some food.
Me: Nice one Marie.
Marie: Thanks and Albert, about black holes emitting gigantic bursts of gamma-rays...
Albert: Yes?
Marie: During one of your calculations a long time ago, one of them рамках burst and caused a chain reaction that utterly destroyed one of your recently built space stations, didn't it?
Albert: ... Yes. That was one of my less convenient inventions. I often experiment on things that shouldn't exist.
Marie: (confused) What do you mean?
Albert: Well, uh... Big screens and lights and things... my famous Black Hole Studio only exists because of that explosion.
Marie: (disgusted) You couldn't keep a black hole in a box, could you?
Albert: It was not a box, Marie. It was a containment field. (softly mutters to himself) More of a containment black hole.
Marie: (unimpressed) Three pinches of salt, Alger.
Albert: Mine to the point that you have to wear a surgical mask.
Marie: (laughs) Alger, you're adorable.
Albert: Marie, that's the most sincere compliment you've ever given me, and it took you 56 years to come out of it.
Marie: I don't have that sort of patience Alger.

Marie gives Albert a long kiss on the cheek. 
Leonardo: Mmm, so Albert... ever met Isaac Newton?
Albert: No, he preceded me by quite a distance.
Marie: (excitedly) But he was the one who predicted black holes, thanks to his laws of motion and universal gravitation.
Albert: That was a very clever prediction, Marie.
Marie: (proudly) It wasn't much of a prediction, Alzheimer.
Albert: It wasn't a prediction. He was right about black holes. He might have been the first.
Leonardo: They're fascinating, really. How do you form one?
Albert: Well, start with an object, any object, even a bowling ball. Squish it down to a point, and it's dense enough to trap light. Light is stuck in the space that's less than the radius of the event horizon.
Marie: It's an excellent idea, Alzheimer, but if you're trying to create a black hole out of a bowling ball, be sure to wear a gas mask.

Marie gives Albert a long kiss on the cheek.
Albert: Now you're getting it, Marie. Black holes are something I'm well-acquainted with.
Marie: Yes, of course, Alzheimer. By the way, are those things we see flying over your head for protection?
Albert: (stressed) Not protection, Marie. They're Avenger drones. They're for the black holes.
Marie: Oh, avengers for black holes? Just between you and me, Alger, you scare the living daylights out of everyone who comes across one of your inventions.
Albert: (shocked) We don't scare anyone, Marie. We're here to help!
Marie: (smiling) Oh, of course, Alger.

Marie gives Albert a long kiss on the cheek.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite President Abraham Lincoln, Sir Isaac Newton, and Beyoncé.

As the guests I've chosen are from vastly different eras, I would alternatively invite them to share a meal and discuss cultural legacies, scientific advancements, and art.

President Abraham Lincoln, a leader who lived through some of the most tumultuous times in American history, could share his insights on leadership, purpose, and democracy. His wisdom and eloquence would certainly add depth to our conversation.

Sir Isaac Newton, one of the most prominent scientists in the history of physics, would undoubtedly add a level of intellectual rigor to our dinner party. His groundbreaking work on the laws of motion and gravity, as well as his contributions to mathematics, would certainly prompt many enlightening discussions.

Beyoncé, the Queen of Pop and one of the most successful female artists of our time, would bring a much-needed dose of joy and inspiration to our dinner. Her impact on culture, music, and the arts cannot be overstated, and I would love to hear her thoughts on her journey and the role of art in society. 

I would look forward to hearing these great minds share their thoughts and experiences, as well as taking advantage of the opportunity to explore new ideas and perspectives.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Here's a list of figures I'd invite:

Plato
Albert Einstein
John F. Kennedy
Edmund Hillary
Mahatma Gandhi
Eleanor Roosevelt
Malala Yousafzai
Nicolaus Copernicus

Plato, because the ideas of his Republic continue to shape how we think about government and society.

Albert Einstein to talk about his theories of relativity and his ideas on pacifism.

John F. Kennedy as an example of a charismatic and effective president, and to ask him how he dealt with the pressures of the Cuban Missile Crisis.

Edmund Hillary for his incredible feat of being the first person to climb Mount Everest, and to hear about his experiences and motivations.

Mahatma Gandhi to hear his thoughts on non-violent resistance and his influence on the Indian independence movement and the U.S. civil rights movement.

Eleanor Roosevelt on her role in championing human rights and her impact on gender and racial equality.

Malala Yousafzai to talk about her struggles and the importance of education for girls around the world.

Nicolaus Copernicus because his revolutionary idea that the Earth revolves around the sun revolutionized astronomy and our understanding of the universe.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
Albert Einstein, Katharine Hepburn, Frieda Lawrence, Rose Wilder Lane, and Konrad Adenauer.

Figures from history come to dinner. We sit around a round table, surrounded by candlelight. I serve a simple meal of some baked chicken, a mixed green salad with a tangy vinaigrette, some steamed asparagus and rice pilaf. I pass around glasses of white wine. 

Albert Einstein looks over his glasses at the meal. He takes a bite, frowns, then nods in approval.

Katharine Hepburn chomps her chicken happily, grinning at me. She has stars in her eyes as she wonders how all of us got here.

Frieda Lawrence looks at the table, at us, then back at the table. Her eyes light up when she sees my face and for a moment, I think she's thrilled to see me. Her smile wavers and then fades. She looks around the table, lost. She breaks into a small, sad smile as she notices me. 

Rose Wilder Lane's carefully cultivated image rests in the chair beside Frieda, but she's not here. Perhaps she's resting from the strain of getting here and still trying to maintain her persona. Or perhaps she's overcome with the emotions of seeing so many people she thought were gone forever.

Konrad Adenauer drinks some wine, muttering to himself, addressing some question he thinks I might have asked. He walks around the table, explaining something to himself, or more likely to me. "I had to learn a few things. About me. About them. About the world." He stops beside me and the table and holds out a glass of wine. "After all, you're not joining me in the afterlife, are you?" 

The table is quiet for a moment.

Einstein chimes in, "He has a point. We should be grateful for the time we have, the lessons we learned, and the people we influenced." He swirls his wine, then crosses his arms over the table. "I'm not sure I agree, though. I think we should have a meeting of the minds, a discussion of what we've done and what could be done to make the world a better place." He lines up some of our statements, Koran quotes, we philosophize for hours. 

Frieda is suddenly awake, fierce and sharp. She thrusts her chair backwards, grasping Rose's chair - fiercely. "Did you hear that?" she hisses. "I thought I was the only one who was paying attention!" 

The group lapses into a polite silence. 

Katharine screams into Frieda's ear, "What, Frieda? Did you hear spirits? You are the extinction of the world. There are no spirits. You know me. You know the rules!"

Frieda begins to sob, and Katharine slams her down softly, then chews her out for being a paranoid, crazy old woman. 

Einstein groans at us, "Can't we get a little more respect?" Ethan Hardy, does his thing. 

Katharine slaps Frieda again, harder this time. Frieda is hit in the face, and she sinks into her chair. The room falls silent for a few beats.

Katharine, ever the performer, looks around the room and all of the eyes are on her. She clutches her heart and gasps for breath. "Only one other thing, folks, and then I'll go. Stand up for yourselves. Stand up for me." She stands up, raises her hands, and starts going into the bowed stance, training a group of children to do a play.

Suddenly there is a loud bang. The table falls. We all plummet to the floor.  

It's asleep again.

Konrad and Rose are exhausted, and they wander off, leaving us all alone again. Albert stumbles off to get water. Katharine and Frieda are left to their bitter cries.

question: Pick a set of figures from history for your own special dinner party where you get to talk to them.
answer:
I would invite Marie Curie, Albert Einstein, and Napoleon Bonaparte. 

Marie Curie, a French born physicist and chemist, was indispensable to the development of X-rays, radiotherapy, and radiography. At the early age of 26, she became the youngest woman and the only woman to win a Nobel Prize in Physics. In addition, she also earned the Nobel Prize in Chemistry, becoming the first person to receive a Nobel in two different sciences. 

Albert Einstein, a German-born theoretical physicist who developed the theory of relativity, is the most cited scientist of all time. His work involved physics, mathematics, and philosophy. Born in 1879, Einstein was also a political activist promoting pacifism and equality.

Lastly, Napoleon Bonaparte is an influential military and political leader whose rule profoundly shaped modern Europe. As a military genius, he led many successful campaigns and defeated the Austrian army and the coalition of several European powers.

It would be great to have these three extraordinary individuals at my own special dinner party.
